Notes、Endnotes、Notes on the Text、Notes to the Chapters、Chapter Notes 这类尾注容器可额外标 endnotes_candidate=true；7. Notes to Chapter 1、Notes to Introduction、或尾注容器下按章分组的子条目可额外标 endnotes_subentry_candidate=true；8. 不要补全不可见文字，只做空白归一化。

llm_traces.reason_for_request: 根据整份目录页重建目录树，并识别尾注容器与子项
llm_traces.request_prompt:
你会依次看到一整份书籍目录页截图。请按整份目录的真实组织方式输出 JSON 对象，不要只做扁平标题提取。输出格式必须是 {"endnotes_summary":{"present":false,"container_title":null,"container_printed_page":null,"container_visual_order":null,"has_chapter_keyed_subentries_in_toc":false,"subentry_pattern":null},"items":[{"title":"章节标题","depth":0,"visual_order":1,"printed_page":12,"role_hint":"chapter","parent_title":"Part One"}]}。规则：1. 必须保留无页码的容器层，例如 Part、Book、COURS、INDICES、APPENDICES；这些条目是 container，不是 chapter。2. chapter 是正文里最独立的可导航单元：显式编号章，以及有正文页码的 Introduction、Conclusion、Préambule、单独 Epilogue。chapter 挂在容器下时仍然是 chapter，不会因为容器存在而变成 section。3. front_matter 只用于第一条正文 chapter 之前的辅助材料，如 Acknowledgments、Remerciements、Preface、Foreword、List of Abbreviations、List of Illustrations。4. back_matter 只用于书末参考性材料，如 Bibliography、References、Index、Indices、Works Cited，以及单行独立的 Note on Sources。5. post_body 只用于最后一条正文 chapter 之后、back_matter 之前的实质性内容，如 RÉSUMÉ、SITUATION、Postambule、独立带页码的 Appendix。6. 书末独立的 Notes、Endnotes、Notes on the Text、Notes to the Chapters、Chapter Notes 是 endnotes 容器，不改变正文 chapter 的层级基准；Bibliography、References、Index 永远不要标成 endnotes。7. Note on Sources 只有在下方有按章 notes 子项时才允许判成 endnotes；如果只是单行独立条目，固定判成 back_matter。8. 如果目录里列出了尾注容器下按章分组的子条目，这些子条目标成 section，parent_title 写尾注容器标题。9. 目录页总标题如 Contents、Sommaire 不输出；标题下方的说明性斜体副标题、摘要句、时间说明如果不是独立可导航标题，不输出。10. parent_title 只写直接父标题；最顶层为空字符串；没有可见页码就把 printed_page 设为 null；请保持从前到后、从上到下的视觉顺序。已抽到的目录标题草稿如下，请优先沿用可见标题原文和顺序，但可以补回漏掉的容器层：
- Mad acts, mad speech, and mad people in Chinese medicine and law
- Prologue
- Psycho-behavioral pathology in Chinese medicine
- From Wind to mucus and Fire
- “Danxi said…”
- Replenish or attack?
- Shapes of practices
- Ghosts or mucus?
- Gendered mania
- Prologue
- 11. Errors and projections
- 12. Punish, but how?
- 13. Life trials
- 14. Distress and power
- 15. Madmen talking?
- 16. Madness multiple
- Bibliographies
- Appendices

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

cluster.chapter_title: From Wind to mucus and Fire

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Hemiplegia caused by Wind strokes [occurs] because Cardiac Fire abruptly becomes excessive, and [Kidney] Water decays and is unable to curb it. Fire can thus overcome Metal, as a result of which Metal can no longer overcome Wood: Liver Wood thus becomes excessive of its own and joins with the heat of the Fire, causing [the] sudden and violent collapse [that is characteristic of Wind strokes]. Sudden illnesses often result from excesses in the Five Wills and Seven Emotions. $ ^{9} $

Using the compelling conceptual architecture of the wuyun liuqi, the Yuanbing shi seemed to make sense of everything: why vomit is acidic, why we have runny noses, why plump persons suffer from more strokes, why some people speak in their sleep, why we laugh when we are tickled, and even why we don't laugh when we tickle ourselves. $ ^{10} $

mechanisms of illness”聞病機何如. $ ^{11} $ Qibo traced one illness each to the five Yin viscera, one each to the lower and upper body, and twelve to various aspects of Qi: five (including kuang) to Fire, four to Heat, and one each to Wind, Dampness, and Cold.

was not air, but an internal form of Qi that appeared mostly because of internally generated Heat and acted in concert with Heat to produce morbid symptoms. As he would state in a later work, “what we speak of as ‘Wind’ is [in fact] ‘Heat’” (凡言風者熱也), because “Wind originally is born from Heat; Heat is the root, Wind the top branch [the superficial manifestation]” (風本生於熱 以熱為本 以風為標). $ ^{14} $

A reader of Liu's treatise would have had no inkling that madness had once been classified as a "Wind illness." Following the passage from the Basic Questions that he was commenting upon, Liu discussed madness under the "Fire" section, where he gave kuang a strong moral meaning: "kuang' means wild unruliness, with no correct or fixed [behavior]; 'yuè' means violating rites and laws and having lost one's normal state" (狂者 狂亂而無正定 越者 乖越禮法而失常也). $ ^{15} $

onset of madness as follows: “Kidney-Water governs the Will, and Water and Fire are opposed to each other. Therefore when Cardiac Fire is overabundant, Renal Water decays: one thus ‘loses one’s Will’ and becomes crazy” (臀水主志而水火相反 故心火旺則腎水衰 乃失志而狂越). $ ^{16} $ Liu also thought that excessive emotions caused by Heat or Fire could trigger madness. $ ^{17} $

Liu Wansu evaluated at some length two sources that disagreed on the nature of the association of dian and kuang: the $ 20^{th} $ problem of the Canon of Problems, and one of Wang Bing's annotations to the Basic Questions (see 3.4). $ ^{18} $ Since neither the $ 20^{th} $ problem nor Wang Bing's commentary mentioned the symptoms of dian and kuang, Liu Wansu could adduce or dispute either passage no matter what meaning he gave to dian. His interpretation of dian and kuang as two similar disorders was widely adopted in Ming medicine, when the great seventh- and eighth-century treatises that had described dian as an epileptiform illness had stopped being reprinted.

It is said that ‘the doubling of Yang is kuang and the doubling of Yin is dian’, but this differs from the explanation found in the Basic Questions. Anger is the emotion associated with the Liver. When Fire is overreplete it curbs Metal, which cannot appease Wood [the Agent associated with the Liver]. rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Three Therapies (Shixing sanliao 十形三療), a selection of medical files compiled by Zhang's disciples shortly after his death. Four of the six cases under “the inner-harm configuration” (内傷形) showed Zhang using counter-affect therapy. $ ^{54} $ Applying his idea that fright would disappear if the patient became accustomed to what scared her, Zhang arranged for people to hit the windows and door of a woman who fell unconscious every time she heard a big noise. $ ^{55} $ After diagnosing that a different woman's two years of insomnia originated in her pensiveness, Zhang did everything he could to infuriate her: he charged more fees than usual, came drunk to the consultation, and departed without making a prescription, leaving his patient sweating with outrage. $ ^{56} $ That night and for several days in a row, she slept soundly and eventually recovered. Anger had overcome pensiveness.

Anger could thus overcome excessive pensiveness, the affect associated with the Spleen. $ ^{52} $ Anger could itself be assuaged by sorrow, the emotion correlated with the Lungs and Metal (the agent that was said to conquer Wood). The two forms of Qi that did not fit into this scheme were fatigue, which should be soothed with idleness (yi 逸), and fright, which was countered by habit (xi 習). $ ^{53} $

What Charlotte Furth has called the “art of diagnosis” is best understood as the art of designing prescriptions on the basis of case-specific diagnoses. $ ^{67} $ The flexible articulation of a prescription to a diagnosis is so often treated as a natural feature of “traditional Chinese medicine” that its historical origins are usually ignored. $ ^{68} $ Zhang and his disciples were in fact among the first to put this principle at the center of their doctrines. $ ^{69} $ Before them, the default mode of prescription (typified by the Song formularies that became their favorite foils) had been to prescribe ready-made remedies for predefined illnesses. Zhang Yuansu and his followers criticized contemporaries for not distinguishing between Cold and Heat or between depletion and repletion, and for consequently failing to prescribe the right remedies in specific illness cases. $ ^{70} $ They also criticized books like the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service,

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: processes and to intervene effectively in these processes. Doctors claimed that yunqi schemes helped them to understand the infinite transformations of illness and to know how to reestablish the dynamic balance that defined good health. Yunqi doctrines became the rage in medical circles soon after the imperial edition of the Basic Questions appeared in the late 11 $ ^{th} $ century, and reached a golden age when they were patronized by Huizong, who considered these cosmic cycles as key to health, government, and cosmic harmony (Yan Liang 2000). $ ^{4} $ Like so many practitioners from the late eleventh century onwards, Liu Wansu treated the yunqi chapters as the most important in the Basic Questions. He even claimed that "the essential of medical teachings lies in the wnyun liuqi" (醫教要乎五運六氣), and made this emphasis evident in his Treatise on the Essential Purport of Yunqi in the Inner Canon (Neijing yunqi yaozhi lun 內經運氣要旨論). $ ^{5} $ Liu's preface (Liu WYQ: 195) to this book stressed how subtle and confounding this system of transformations could be to the non-initiated. Few indeed could awaken to its meaning (悟得其意者鮮矣). In several of his works, Liu called wuyun liuqi a “Mysterious Mechanism” (玄機), from a Taoist cosmological term that did not itself appear in the Inner Canon. $ ^{6} $ This notion

became the mainstay of Liu's most influential book: the Method for Tracing Illnesses to their Origins with the Mysterious Mechanism of the Basic Questions (Suwen xuanji yuanbing shi 素問玄機原 病式; hereafter Yuanbing shi).⁷ There, Liu alleged that “in the method for distinguishing illnesses, it can be seen clearly that the Qi of [all] illnesses can be reduced to transformations of the five phases and six Qi” (識病之法 以其病氣歸於五運六氣之化 明可見矣). Liu Wansu used the fluid interactions between the Five Agents as a guide for scripting both physiological and pathological processes. $ ^{8} $ The Five Agents and the five Yin viscera (五臟) interacted in a way illustrated synoptically in Figure 4.1 (next page). This system was homeostatic (it had an inherent tendency to equilibrium) but dynamic. Since all of its elements were inter-related, a slight imbalance (be it excess or deficiency) in any of the Five Agents or organs led to illnesses that could affect the entire organism. By a sequence of effects predicted by the model, an excess of emotions could, for instance, lead to hemiplegia: Hemiplegia caused by Wind strokes [occurs] because Cardiac Fire abruptly becomes excessive, and [Kidney] Water decays and is unable to curb it. Fire can thus overcome Metal, as a result of which Metal can no longer overcome Wood: Liver Wood thus becomes excessive of its own and joins with the heat of the Fire, causing [th ...

By explaining illness on the plane of the Five Agents, Liu severed the causal chain from the organs themselves.
Fig. 4.1 Mutual conquest and mutual production cycles of the Five Agents and their associated viscera. (This diagram represents the system in a balanced state.)
Using the compelling conceptual architecture of the wuyun liuqi, the Yuanbing shi seemed to make sense of everything: why vomit is acidic, why we have runny noses, why plump persons suffer from more strokes, why some people speak in their sleep, why we laugh when we are tickled, and even why we don't laugh when we tickle ourselves. $ ^{10} $ Liu's ambitious treatise was in fact an extensive commentary on a passage from chapter 74 of the Basic Questions (one of the seven "Grand treatises") where Qibo explained the origins of 19 disorders to satisfy the Yellow Emperor's wish to "hear about the

mechanisms of illness”聞病機何如. $ ^{11} $ Qibo traced one illness each to the five Yin viscera, one each to the lower and upper body, and twelve to various aspects of Qi: five (including kuang) to Fire, four to Heat, and one each to Wind, Dampness, and Cold. Extrapolating that the five illnesses the canonical excerpt attributed to the viscera actually originated in the movements of Fire, Liu Wansu took this passage as authoritative proof of the pathological power of Heat and Fire, which now represented 14 out of the original 19 entries—five from the viscera, four from Heat, and five from Fire. The short first section on “the five circulatory phases [as they] govern illness” (五運主病) (1a-3b) discussed the Five Agents as they succeeded each other during the year (Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, and Water). The rest of the work was devoted to “the six Qi [as they] bring about illness” (六氣為病). Heat (4b-31a) and Fire (33a-57b) made up almost three quarters of the entire book. $ ^{12} $ Liu took the six Qi not as climatic influences (their traditional meaning), but largely as manifestations of internal imbalances. The close attention he paid to internal pathological processes makes him representative of a larger trend in Chinese medical thinking that I started to explore in the previous chapter when I showed that Song physicians increasingly explained Wind illnesses by internal processes govern ...

was not air, but an internal form of Qi that appeared mostly because of internally generated Heat and acted in concert with Heat to produce morbid symptoms. As he would state in a later work, “what we speak of as ‘Wind’ is [in fact] ‘Heat’” (凡言風者熱也), because “Wind originally is born from Heat; Heat is the root, Wind the top branch [the superficial manifestation]” (風本生於熱 以熱為本 以風為標). $ ^{14} $ Liu Wansu's descriptions of the pathogenesis of a large number of illnesses gave a central place to internal Fire and Heat (instead of Wind). Liu's focus on Heat also explains his predilection for the use of cooling drugs, many of which he drew from the Treatise on Cold Injury. Liu's reinterpretation of Wind as inner Heat contributed to the collapse of the notion of "Wind illnesses" (see sections 4.2, 4.4, and 4.7 below), which in turn helped kuang to become one among many "miscellaneous illnesses" (雜病) that could be discussed independently. A reader of Liu's treatise would have had no inkling that madness had once been classified as a "Wind illness." onset of madness as follows: “Kidney-Water governs the Will, and Water and Fire are opposed to each other. Therefore when Cardiac Fire is overabundant, Renal Water decays: one thus ‘loses one’s Will’ and becomes crazy” (臀水主志而水火相反 故心火旺則腎水衰 乃失志而狂越). $ ^{16} $ Liu also thought that excessive emotions caused by Heat or Fire could trigger madness. $ ^{17} $ Liu Wansu evaluated at some length two sources that disagreed on the nature of the association of dian and kuang: the $ 20^{th} $ problem of the Canon of Problems, and one of Wang Bing's annotations to the Basic Questions (see 3.4). $ ^{18} $ Since neither the $ 20^{th} $ problem nor Wang Bing's commentary mentioned the symptoms of dian and kuang, Liu Wansu could adduce or dispute either passage no matter what meaning he gave to dian. kuang. Since all five emotions generate Heat, kuang is triggered by any of the five emotions. It is simply that anger is [a] more common [cause]. $ ^{20} $ 或云重陽者狂 重陰者癲 則素問之說不同也 經注曰 多喜為癲 多怒為狂 然喜為心志 故心熱甚則多喜而為癲也 怒為肝志 火實制金不能平木故肝實則多怒而為狂也 況五志所發皆為熱 故狂者五志間發 但怒多爾 After having cited many passages from the Basic Questions that were compatible with his own view of emotions as manifestations of Fire and of madness as caused by inner Heat, Liu concluded with a rhetorical question aimed to refute the Canon of Problems: “Can it really be, then, that dian is the doubling of Yin?” (夫豈癲為重陰者歟) Liu Wansu's interpretations were not grounded in his (or anybody's) observation of patients. His reasoning relied on textual authority and on deduction following the association of the five viscera with the Five Agents and the emotions. Bypassing the entire "Wind illness" tradition, Liu confirmed his Heat etiology by citing passages from the Treatise on Cold Injury—another work he greatly admired—that attributed disorders of the Yang-brilliance tract to Heat. $ ^{21} $ I know of no major medical work after Liu's Yuanbing shi 原病式 that attributed madness to Wind, whereas Heat and Fire remained important identified causes of psycho-behavioral disorders until the end of imperial history. Later physicians also took Liu Wansu's comparison of the Canon of Problems with Wang Bing's later annotation as an argument fo ...

kuang in symptoms (instead of an epileptiform illness) took place only in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, a process I shall describe in more detail in sections 6.5 and 6.6. $ ^{22} $ ### 4.2 Zhang Congzheng: convergences and crystallizations Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228), from Kaocheng 考城 in Suizhou 睢州 (Henan), was the first Chinese physician to attribute madness to Heat and mucus explicitly and to treat it consistently with purgatives and emetics. He also systematized counter-affect therapy, a kind of treatment that came to be discussed next to madness in the sixteenth century (6.4). Zhang's doctrines were inspired by Liu Wansu, but also by the Basic Questions, the Treatise on Cold Injury, and a variety of Song physicians whose books Zhang had read. unmatched_anchors.source_text: Qing Suzhou was an important center of medical studies supported by a prosperous printing industry and mature scholarly networks. As one of the main cities on the Grand Canal, it had long been an important hub of trade and communication, and many doctors had traveled there to find books and exchange ideas. But in Qing times Suzhou physicians claimed to represent the most advanced medical approaches in the empire. Although this pride predated the eighteenth century, it became particularly evident after the publication in 1766 of some of Ye Gui's 葉桂 (1667-1746; see 7.5 below) case files by men who claimed to be his disciples. I discuss Suzhou doctors' approach to madness in the next chapter. $ ^{10} $

with warming drugs, others kept blaming them for it, as when Xu Yuhe 許豫和 (late 18 $ ^{th} $ century) accused a physician of having exacerbated a madman’s symptoms with calorific replenishing, or when Wu Tang 吴塘 (1758-1836) claimed that another doctor’s attacking of mucus and Fire in a case of insanity had “helped the bandits do harm” (助賊為虐). $ ^{11} $

Although mucus and Fire had long been paired, Liang Xuemeng 梁學孟 in 1607 was perhaps the first to dedicate a section to “mucus Fire” (痰火) in a medical work (his Guoyi zongzhi 國醫宗旨). $ ^{12} $ His Tanbuo zhuanmen 痰火顓門 (1610) became the first work devoted entirely to this topic, $ ^{13} $ followed by Gong Juzhong’s 龔居中 Tanbuo dianxue 痰火點雪 (1630). Zhang Lu also discussed mucus-Fire in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通, 1695), explaining that “formful [i.e., visible and palpable] mucus” (有形之痰) was associated with “formless [invisible] Fire” (無形之火). $ ^{14} $ Cao Cunxin 曹存心 (1767-1834, from Changshu 常熟 in Jiangsu), later posed mucus and Fire as two equivalents: “Mucus is none other than Fire with form, whereas Fire is formless mucus” (痰即有形之火 火即無形之痰). $ ^{15} $

Zhang Lu also discussed mucus-Fire in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通, 1695), explaining that “formful [i.e., visible and palpable] mucus” (有形之痰) was associated with “formless [invisible] Fire” (無形之火). $ ^{14} $ Cao Cunxin 曹存心 (1767-1834, from Changshu 常熟 in Jiangsu), later posed mucus and Fire as two equivalents: “Mucus is none other than Fire with form, whereas Fire is formless mucus” (痰即有形之火 火即無形之痰). $ ^{15} $

During the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, physicians increasingly asserted the general importance of mucus pathology. Zhang Lu claimed that mucus appeared in seven or eight ill people out of ten (痰飲為患 十人有七八), $ ^{16} $ whereas Cheng Guopeng 程國彭 (from She 歙 county in Huizhou; fl. early 18 $ ^{th} $ century) asserted that “There are no illnesses in which there is no fever or mucus” (凡病未有不發熱 不生痰者). $ ^{17} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: These two illnesses [dian and kuang] necessarily arise from something. It can be great anger stirring Hepatic Wind or great fright stirring Cardiac Fire, making Fire rise suddenly. When [Fire] arises but fails to descend, it obstructs the openings of the Heart and makes Spiritual-clarity $ ^{32} $ unable to circulate in and out; the sovereign loses his [power to] issue commands and the Heart is instead conscripted by mucus; [this process] immediately [makes the illness] manifest itself.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: to study Huangfu Zhong's original Enlightened Physician (1579) in order to understand Zhang Ji's Cold-Injury medicine, Li Gao's notion of inner harm, Liu Wansu's Heat disorders, and Zhu Zhenheng's miscellaneous illnesses. $ ^{26} $ As Shao recalled, he learned as much as he could from Huangfu's work, but deplored that it relied on illustrations (圖) at the expense of discussions (論), described the symptoms clearly but said little about pulses, and gave detailed recipes but neglected to explain the therapeutic methods that justified this choice of remedies. $ ^{27} $ Shao also found inconvenient that the recipes appeared in a volume separate from the descriptions and illustrations. $ ^{28} $ In his 1622 edition, Shao eliminated the pictures, regrouped the descriptions of illnesses with the recommended recipes, and added distinctions between the many possible origins of every illness he discussed.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: If one scales walls and climbs on roofs, tries to kill people with a knife, strips naked and shouts around without discrimination between kin and kith, scampers around rapidly, $ ^{33} $ and walks in water as if it were soil; it is because Hepatic Qi is overly dominant and Wood comes to straddle the Heart. This is called knang or “severe dian.” $ ^{34} $ The method is to repress the Liver and pacify the Heart, chiefly with the “Elixir that brings down the dragon.” $ ^{35} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Gong’s characterization of dian as calm and unconscious and kuang as agitated and awake was in line with the shift in the meaning of dian that I describe in 7.6 below. The Guide to Clinical Practice distinguished between dian, kuang, and xian along the lines that stabilized in modern times: kuang as mania, dian as mostly quiet, and xian as epileptiform. But Gong also retained part of the older approaches when he associated kuang with the Liver (tied to anger) and dian with the Heart (linked with joy). He interpreted dian’s millennial association with Yin (see 3.4) as dian’s grounding in three Yin tracts. $ ^{92} $ Section 7.6 will explain what other physicians said of this Yin-Yang classification of dian and kuang, but for now let me show

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Physicians had long associated kuang with anger, the Hepatic emotion. $ ^{93} $ Doctors sometimes re-iterated the ancient claim that the “cloud souls” (魂), which were partly responsible for mental activities, resided in the Liver. $ ^{94} $ But these correlations had not noticeably shaped the way in which physicians explained or treated mad behavior. In the eighteenth and nineteenth century, however, medical writers gave increasing importance to Liver imbalances in the etiology of mad behavior and many other disorders. These changes were not revolutionary—because most literati doctors still couched their explanations within the conceptual framework of the Five Agents, continued to rely on pulse-taking as their main diagnostic method, and prescribed mostly decoctions to perform cures—but the transformations I describe are so little-known that I would rather focus on them than emphasize the undeniable continuity of Chinese medical practice at the time.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: Xiuyuan 陳修園 (1753-1823) also adopted Zhang Lu's new interpretation of the “four masters” in his Three-Character Classic of Medicine (Yixue san zì jìng 醫學三字經; 1804), pointing out that Li Zhongzi's interpretation of them as including Zhang Ji was “erroneous” (誤). $ ^{9} $ ## Other Qing trends Qing Suzhou was an important center of medical studies supported by a prosperous printing industry and mature scholarly networks. As one of the main cities on the Grand Canal, it had long been an important hub of trade and communication, and many doctors had traveled there to find books and exchange ideas. But in Qing times Suzhou physicians claimed to represent the most advanced medical approaches in the empire. Although this pride predated the eighteenth century, it became particularly evident after the publication in 1766 of some of Ye Gui's 葉桂 (1667-1746; see 7.5 below) case files by men who claimed to be his disciples. I discuss Suzhou doctors' approach to madness in the next chapter. $ ^{10} $ One trend in the eighteenth and especially the nineteenth century was the rising number of case collections in the surviving medical literature. Many of them were never printed. They allow us to continue comparing the propositions found in treatises with the way physicians actually handled mad patients. Through them, we can also understand better how women were treated when they behaved in ways t ...

with warming drugs, others kept blaming them for it, as when Xu Yuhe 許豫和 (late 18 $ ^{th} $ century) accused a physician of having exacerbated a madman’s symptoms with calorific replenishing, or when Wu Tang 吴塘 (1758-1836) claimed that another doctor’s attacking of mucus and Fire in a case of insanity had “helped the bandits do harm” (助賊為虐). $ ^{11} $ Medical writing also remained profoundly intertextual. Several treatises from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries included long and increasingly complex sections on madness. They were longer and denser partly because they could borrow from a larger number of texts, but also because they applied to more disorders than ever the old claim that any illness could be caused by multiple factors. Two representative examples are Feng Zhaozhang's 馮兆張 (from Haiyan 海鹽 in Zhejiang) Jinnang milu zazheng daxiao becan 錦囊祕錄雜證大小合參 (in 20 juan; 1694) and Shen Jin'ao's 沈金鰲 (1717-1776; from Wuxi 無錫 in Jiangsu) Zabing yuanliu xizhu 雜病源流犀燭 (in 30 juan; 1773), both of which concentrated on explaining miscellaneous illnesses. Besides these exhaustive accounts of manifold etiologies, we find attempts to simplify—even unify—medical doctrines, books with titles like Unity in the Forest of Medicine (Luo Denggao's 駱登高 Yilin yizhi 醫林一致; 1703), Knowing Medicine in One Glance (Zhu Shijin's 朱時進 Yijian nengyi 一見能醫; 1769), and Medicine for Sots (Long Huitang's ...

### 7.2 The continued dominance of the mucus etiology In earlier chapters, I examined the rise of mucus in Chinese medical thinking and its eventual use in explanations of madness. I also showed how the Danxi corpus had given pride of place to the mucus etiology in general. On the basis of these now mature theories, seventeenth-century physicians continued to discuss how mucus emerged, what disorders it caused, and how these illnesses should be treated. Some did so in special sections on this viscous intruder, others in commentaries on the notion of “mucus and rheum” (痰飲; 3.5) in Zhang Ji’s Essentials of the Golden Casket (3 $ ^{rd} $ century CE). Although mucus and Fire had long been paired, Liang Xuemeng 梁學孟 in 1607 was perhaps the first to dedicate a section to “mucus Fire” (痰火) in a medical work (his Guoyi zongzhi 國醫宗旨). $ ^{12} $ His Tanbuo zhuanmen 痰火顓門 (1610) became the first work devoted entirely to this topic, $ ^{13} $ followed by Gong Juzhong’s 龔居中 Tanbuo dianxue 痰火點雪 (1630). Zhang Lu also discussed mucus-Fire in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通, 1695), explaining that “formful [i.e., visible and palpable] mucus” (有形之痰) was associated with “formless [invisible] Fire” (無形之火). $ ^{14} $ Cao Cunxin 曹存心 (1767-1834, from Changshu 常熟 in Jiangsu), later posed mucus and Fire as two equivalents: “Mucus is none other than Fire with form, whereas Fire is formless mucus” (痰 ...

During the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, physicians increasingly asserted the general importance of mucus pathology. Zhang Lu claimed that mucus appeared in seven or eight ill people out of ten (痰飲為患 十人有七八), $ ^{16} $ whereas Cheng Guopeng 程國彭 (from She 歙 county in Huizhou; fl. early 18 $ ^{th} $ century) asserted that “There are no illnesses in which there is no fever or mucus” (凡病未有不發熱 不生痰者). $ ^{17} $ This perceived ubiquity of mucus generated more explanations of its pathological origins. Physicians argued that mucus could stem from an almost infinite diversity of imbalances and failures to circulate. $ ^{18} $ Huang Yuanyu 黄元御 (1705-1758) grounded mucus in Dampness (associated with the Spleen), another common explanation. $ ^{19} $ Chen Xiuyuan 陳修園 (1753-1823), a popularizer of medical doctrines whose writings circulated widely in the second half of the nineteenth century, claimed that mucus was rooted in Water (the phase associated with either the two Kidneys or the left Kidney) and that sticky mucus appeared when Water Qi was “decocted” by excessive Yang (得陽煎熬). $ ^{20} $

### 7.3 Shapes of practice: the scope of mucus therapies of madness The above section showed that mucus played an important role in Qing medical discussions of many illnesses. Starting with an examination of seventeenth-century medical primers that were popular under the Qing, let me now demonstrate from many different angles that mucus conceptions also dominated the way doctors thought about insanity. Since the practice of medicine was not regulated, virtually anyone could take it up. All that was needed was a medical primer and a few patients willing to pay for a cure. Physicians who sought a more elite clientele usually needed to claim a teacher and to be able to couch their arguments in classical terms drawn from the medical canons or the Yijing, but the success of medical primers suggests that medicine was easily learned outside of teacher-student relations. The large number of surviving editions of books like Huangfu Zhong's 皇甫中 Enlightened Physician Pointing to His Palm (Mingyi zhizhang 明醫指掌) or Gong Tingxian's Shoushi baoyuan 壽世保元, and the fact that other books lamented their excessive influence, suggest that they guided the practice of more physicians than the treatises modern historians rely on in their studies of Chinese medical thinking. $ ^{21} $ ## Shapers of practice? What seventeenth-century medical manuals said about madness There is no lack of people within an ...

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Zbezbong is difficult to translate. One of the earliest meanings of zbe 折 was “to break” or “to snap.” In legal language, zbe yu 折獄 meant “judging [i.e., deciding] a criminal case.” $ ^{6} $ But zbe in zbe yu also referred to the weighing and pondering that led to the final decision. $ ^{7} $ Zbe zbong (折中 or 折衷) itself implied the balanced assessment of various positions. $ ^{8} $ We may gloss zbezbong as to resolve—a case, a point of doctrine, etc.—by arbitrating between various positions, often on the basis of another authority. $ ^{9} $

Intertextual analysis also reveals why medical knowledge was not simply “passed on” from one generation to the other. It shows that the illness scripts that interest us were more than culturally informed responses to observed symptoms. These scripts also emerged and developed intertextually. Finally, the zbezzhong mode of interpreting the past largely explains why Chinese medical thinking often appears so “syncretistic” and “integrative.” $ ^{10} $

The result of zbezzhong writing was not a simple average of previous teachings, it was a coherent amalgamation of several approaches to illness. Zbezzhong was deliberate syncretism—-eclecticism by design, not by default. Focusing on the intertextuality inherent in the zbezzhong approach avoids treating changes in medical doctrine as radical departures from predecessors or as the unfolding of realizations in the consciousness of a series of unrelated authors. Intertextual analysis also reveals why medical knowledge was not simply “passed on” from one generation to the other. It shows that the illness scripts that interest us were more than culturally informed responses to observed symptoms. These scripts also emerged and developed intertextually. Finally, the zbezzhong mode of interpreting the past largely explains why Chinese medical thinking often appears so “syncretistic” and “integrative.” $ ^{10} $

The only early-Ming treatise that contained a substantial section on madness was Lou Ying's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning (Yixue gangmu). This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $

This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $

This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $

Another reason why Lou took so long to complete his opus was the complexity of its subject matter. Lou opened his preface with a telling statement on “medicine as a learning” (醫之為學): “its Way is extensive, its meaning profound, its books infinitely abundant” (其道博 其義深 其書浩瀚). $ ^{16} $ Although Lou claimed that the essentials of medicine “simply hinge on Yin-Yang and the Five Agents” (其要不過陰陽五行而已), he admitted he took more than thirty years to realize that the myriad transformations of illness emerged from imbalances in Yin-Yang and the Five Agents. $ ^{17} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: This chapter explores the way medical texts were composed and handed down, the history of important medical doctrines, and the formation and transmission of medical ideas on madness. I first analyze the most elaborate section on madness of all pre-1500 Chinese medical treatises—the section on kuang of a book by Zhu Zhenheng's disciple Lou Ying 櫻英 (1320-1400)—to show how an erudite physician selected from a rich textual heritage to construct a coherent view of illness. I then discuss how another illustrious disciple of Zhu's, Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405), contributed to giving prestige to the Danxi approach. Next, I explain how two books that Liu Chun 劉純 (1340-1412), whose father had been Zhu's pupil, compiled in Shaanxi in the late fourteenth century typified the zhezhong approach to the past and further helped to diffused Danxi medicine. In a fourth section, I explain the emergence of the core works of the Danxi corpus in the late fifteenth century and the evolution of their sections on madness. The final two parts are about two writers whose works based on Danxi texts were widely debated and cited in the decades that followed their death. These writers were Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510), from Ningbo, and Yu Tuan 虞搏 (1438-1517), from the same locale as Zhu in Zhejiang. Though I focus on individual writers, I insist that they were responding to each other and explain how each of them proposed different interpretations of a common body of texts that included the Danxi corpus, but also the Inner Canon and the works of Liu Wansu and Li Gao. $ ^{11} $ Throughout, I try to recreate the doctrinal debates that make changes in their approaches to madness intelligible.

(太醫院使, ranked 5a), in which quality Dai served until his retirement in 1403 at the age of 80 sui. $ ^{41} $ Dai was then replaced by Han Mao 韓茂, a Suzhou man Dai had recommended many years before. Dai returned home early in 1404. When Dai went back to the capital in the summer of 1405, the newly enthroned Yongle emperor (r. 1402-1424) sent Dai's former student Yuan Bao, who was now Administrative Assistant of the Imperial Academy of Medicine (太醫院判; the second highest post in the organization), to greet him. $ ^{42} $ Dai died in late 1405, soon after he had returned home again.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Liu and his teachers were products of the Lower Yangzi region, but in the early Hongwu reign (1368-1398), Liu and his family left to establish themselves in Xi'an, the Shaanxi provincial capital. rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Liu Chun's Subtle Meanings contained no section on madness, but the book gave no grounds for attributing kuang to anything else than mucus and Fire. $ ^{64} $ Juan 10 (devoted to Fire) started with Liu Wansu's (4.1) attribution of most illnesses—including kuang—to Fire. The second section was Zhu Zhenheng's "On Ministerial Fire" (4.4); the third cited Dai Sigong's appendices to Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄. $ ^{65} $ The mucus chapter contained a cross-reference to the section on "depletion and fatigue" 虚損 (juan 19) for a discussion of Zhu's idea that depletion and mucus could cause possession symptoms. The text recommended emetics for most disorders attributed to mucus, and purgatives for those caused by Fire. $ ^{66} $ Subtle Meanings of the Secret Carved in Jade was thus squarely part of the intertextual and syncretic Danxi approach to illness.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: own writings. I will also show that $ \underline{\text{zhezhong}} $ hermeneutics and textual composition rested on the notion that illness was constantly changing. Zbezbong is difficult to translate. One of the earliest meanings of zbe 折 was “to break” or “to snap.” In legal language, zbe yu 折獄 meant “judging [i.e., deciding] a criminal case.” $ ^{6} $ But zbe in zbe yu also referred to the weighing and pondering that led to the final decision. $ ^{7} $ Zbe zbong (折中 or 折衷) itself implied the balanced assessment of various positions. $ ^{8} $ We may gloss zbezbong as to resolve—a case, a point of doctrine, etc.—by arbitrating between various positions, often on the basis of another authority. $ ^{9} $ The result of zbezzhong writing was not a simple average of previous teachings, it was a coherent amalgamation of several approaches to illness. Zbezzhong was deliberate syncretism—-eclecticism by design, not by default. Focusing on the intertextuality inherent in the zbezzhong approach avoids treating changes in medical doctrine as radical departures from predecessors or as the unfolding of realizations in the consciousness of a series of unrelated authors. Intertextual analysis also reveals why medical knowledge was not simply “passed on” from one generation to the other. It shows that the illness scripts that interest us were more than culturally informed responses to observed ...

This chapter explores the way medical texts were composed and handed down, the history of important medical doctrines, and the formation and transmission of medical ideas on madness. I first analyze the most elaborate section on madness of all pre-1500 Chinese medical treatises—the section on kuang of a book by Zhu Zhenheng's disciple Lou Ying 櫻英 (1320-1400)—to show how an erudite physician selected from a rich textual heritage to construct a coherent view of illness. I then discuss how another illustrious disciple of Zhu's, Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405), contributed to giving prestige to the Danxi approach. Next, I explain how two books that Liu Chun 劉純 (1340-1412), whose father had been Zhu's pupil, compiled in Shaanxi in the late fourteenth century typified the zhezhong approach to the past and further helped to diffused Danxi medicine. In a fourth section, I explain the emergence of the core works of the Danxi corpus in the late fifteenth century and the evolution of their sections on madness. The final two parts are about two writers whose works based on Danxi texts were widely debated and cited in the decades that followed their death. These writers were Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510), from Ningbo, and Yu Tuan 虞搏 (1438-1517), from the same locale as Zhu in Zhejiang. Though I focus on individual writers, I insist that they were responding to each other and explain how each of them p ...

### 5.2 Madness in Lou Ying's 樓英 (1320-1400) $ ^{12} $ Yixue gangmu 醫學綱目 (1396) The only early-Ming treatise that contained a substantial section on madness was Lou Ying's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning (Yixue gangmu). This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $ Lou Ying started to collect materials for his summa of current medical doctrine and healing methods in the 1360s and completed a first version in 1380; he achieved the final version in 1396. Lou did not have all the books he consulted at hand at any given time. He probably kept adding to his text as he consulted more and more works, something that was made more convenient by the proximity of Hangzhou ( ...

Another reason why Lou took so long to complete his opus was the complexity of its subject matter. Lou opened his preface with a telling statement on “medicine as a learning” (醫之為學): “its Way is extensive, its meaning profound, its books infinitely abundant” (其道博 其義深 其書浩瀚). $ ^{16} $ Although Lou claimed that the essentials of medicine “simply hinge on Yin-Yang and the Five Agents” (其要不過陰陽五行而已), he admitted he took more than thirty years to realize that the myriad transformations of illness emerged from imbalances in Yin-Yang and the Five Agents. $ ^{17} $ Textual montages like Lou's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning were not amateurish endeavors. Composite texts professing to cover all aspects of illness and medicine were actually a new genre of medical writing at which Zhu Zhenheng's disciples excelled. $ ^{18} $ Many books claiming to be for beginners were sophisticated digests of complex medical teachings; they were not easy “primers,” but points of entry into the intricate traditions they summarized. Students of medicine found these compilations all the more useful since readers could not easily access all the texts these works cited. Lou Ying's compilation was concerned with medical practice, not abstract doctrine. As a reasoned anthology of citations, the point of the Systematic Outline of Medical Learning was not to reconcile all medical approaches into a perfectly ...

Zhongjing [Zhang Ji] is detailed on external contamination that affects the interior and the surface or Yin and Yang; Danxi distinguishes himself on inner harm of Blood or Qi [that causes] depletion or repletion; Dongyuan [Li Gao] supports and protects central Qi; Hejian [Liu Wansu] removes the old to create the new; Mr. Qian [Yi] distinguishes clearly between the five Yin viscera; Dairen [Zhang Congzheng] is well versed in the three methods [vomiting, purging, and sweating]. Formularies from past ages are abundant: we can simply say that each has its strengths. 仲景詳外感於表裡陰陽 丹溪獨內傷於血氣虛實 東垣扶護中氣 河間推陳 致新 錢氏分明五臟 戴人熟施三法 凡歷代方書甚衆 皆各有所長耳 Since the same symptoms could occur for different reasons, it was plausible that many texts could contain useful methods of treatment. $ ^{19} $ “The benighted fail to appreciate this principle: their reckless trying [of recipes] on approximate illnesses has led many to an unjust and premature death” (昧者不悟是理 妄試疑似之病 每致夭橫者不少矣). Armed with a definition of illness as a multifaceted and ever-changing entity that was typical of Zhu Zhenheng's followers, $ ^{20} $ Lou proposed an elegant solution to the controversial issue of whether “old recipes” could cure “current illnesses.” “Given that an illness with a single name [can require] different methods of treatment, when people use recipes from past ages to treat illness, their success or failure depends on wheth ...

from the Treatise on Cold Injury (or any other source), for instance, were effective when they were used to treat the kinds of illness they cured best. In typical zbezbong fashion, Lou also used this flexible conception of illness and therapy to justify a syncretic approach to the textual past and to clinical practice. His book counted forty fascicles or chapters. The first nine—all part of a large section called “Yin-Yang and the viscera” 陰陽臟腑部—explained general principles on pathomechanisms, diagnosis, and treatments. Followed twenty fascicles (10-29) describing various illnesses and methods to treat them, four fascicles on Cold Injury (30-3), two on the illnesses of women (34-5), four on those of children (36-9), and one with commentaries on the five periods and six Qi in the Inner Canon (40). $ ^{21} $ In the twenty chapters on disorders other than Cold Injury, Lou Ying classified illnesses into five sections (bu 部) named after the pairs of Yin and Yang viscera with which he associated them. There were six fascicles on the Liver and Gallbladder (肝膽), five on the Heart and Small Intestine (心小腸), five on the Spleen and Stomach (脾胃), two on the Lungs and Large Intestine (肺大腸), and two on the Kidneys and Bladder (腎膀胱). These sections did not present the anatomy of these viscera: they discussed illnesses that occurred when these organs failed to fulfil their functions. The secti ...

records from various sources. $ ^{22} $ Lou shortened and slightly modified some passages, but his own utterances were limited to his introduction (translated above), cross-references to other sections for remedies whose recipes were given elsewhere, and very brief (and themselves intertextual) “comments” (批) after some citations. $ ^{23} $ Lou’s section on kuang introduced a mix of approaches to madness, as well as recipes compiled at different periods that aimed to cure various imbalances. What justified this catholicity of approaches was not a theoretical reflection on madness in particular, but one on illness in general: any illness could be caused by a large array of imbalances and could therefore be treated in a variety of ways. Note nonetheless that Lou gave little room to kuang’s older etiologies—Wind was mentioned only in a “comment” on what Lou considered to be an anti-Wind remedy by Zhang Ji 張機. $ ^{24} $ The Wind etiology appeared discreetly in recipes that were once used to counter Wind strokes, but nothing in Lou’s work signaled that madness had once been considered as a Wind illness. $ ^{25} $ The cases of madness that Lou selected deployed mostly aggressive therapies. $ ^{26} $ Three of Zhang Congzheng's 張從正 four cases had used attacking methods. A case from the Shiyi dexiao fang 世醫得效方 (1337) used powdered melon pedicle (not to be confused with the multi-ingredi ...

cure a female patient. $ ^{28} $ All these methods either became or remained widely used in late- imperial medicine. Only one case deployed an approach that became marginal: Sun Zhao's 孫兆 (late eleventh century) cure of a monk with a recipe and method designed by Shen Kuo 沈括 (1031-1095). $ ^{29} $ These mostly aggressive recipes and methods contrasted with the general therapeutic orientation of the Systematic Outline. Lou's "General discussion on methods of treatment" (Zhifa tonglun 治法通論, beginning of juan 3) cited 21 passages: eight from Wang Haogu, three from Li Gao, three from the Basic Questions, two each from Zhang Ji and Zhu Zhenheng, and one each from the Divine Pivot, the Majue, and the Canon of Problems. $ ^{30} $ Thirteen of the fifteen passages attributed to specific authors were from physicians whose priority, according to Lou, had been "to help and protect primordial Qi" (扶護元氣為主). $ ^{31} $ The contrast between Lou's general advice and the remedies he recommended for treating madness confirms the strength that the Fire and mucus etiology of madness had acquired by the fourteenth century. Madness was then largely seen as an illness caused by some kind of over-repletion. Be it Fire, mucus, Heat, or Wind, it had to be purged or attacked. $ ^{32} $ Lou Ying did not cite Zhu Zhenheng's ideas on madness, probably because he did not find any in the texts by Zhu that he po ...

      "page_no": 232,
      "ocr_excerpt": "### 6.2 Replenishing madness: Wang Ji's case-based reasoning Wang Ji 汪機 (1463-1540), from Qimen 祁門 county in Huizhou, was one of a handful of sixteenth-century physicians who attributed a case of mad behavior to depletion and treated it accordingly. $ ^{23} $ This section outlines the emergence, in the works of Wang and a few more doctors, of a conception of madness caused by depletion that would become more prominent later in the sixteenth century. I first explain the doctrinal background of Wang's predilection for treating patients with ginseng and astragalus root (黄芪), then I present two of his madness cases in light of his broader orientations. Finally I describe the replenishing therapies that other contemporary physicians deployed to treat madness symptoms. Both in his approach to madness and in his general doctrinal discussions, Wang often tackled issues that were discussed in the contemporary medical literature. The larger community of experts of which he was part tackled issues defined by the Danxi corpus (see Chapter 5) and discussed the writings of illustrious authors like Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510; see 5.6). Strongly opposing this admonition, Wang Ji tried to show that calorific replenishers could treat not only cases of Qi depletion (as everyone agreed), but also illnesses that were caused by Blood depletion. Wang Ji had to dispute Wang Lun’s authority in his daily medical practice. unmatched_anchors.source_text: First, despite many claims to the contrary, Wang Ji's work was not the first autonomous work to contain the cases of a single physician. $ ^{4} $ Zhang Congzheng's Ten Configurations and Three Therapies (Shixing sanliao 十形三療), an anthology of Zhang's cases, was edited soon after his death (1228) by his disciples, and was circulating on its own by the fourteenth century. $ ^{5} $ Case records were also an important part of Zhu Zhenheng's medical practice even if he did not collate them into a book during his lifetime. $ ^{6} $ Both his biographer

It is no coincidence that Zhu Zhenheng's disciples and admirers were the leading transmitters of this flexible art of prescription during the early Ming dynasty. Zhu's own case files played a role. Wang Xing's 王行 preface (dated 1377, less than 20 years after Zhu's death) to a compendium of Zhu's cases explained how unusual Zhu's approach had been. $ ^{9} $ Most physicians, Wang claimed, instructed "to treat such-and-such an illness with this remedy, and that such-and-such a remedy treats such-and-such an illness" (治某病以某方以某方治某病而已). But "illnesses change greatly and are without constancy, whereas recipes are fixed and limited" (病多變而無常 方一定而有限). And how could one
“respond to inconstant illnesses with limited recipes”(以有限之方應無常之病)? $ ^{10} $ Wang
received from Dai himself the cases by Zhu that Wang Xing was prefacing. $ ^{11} $

The success of other practitioners in the “Zhu Danxi” line was also explained by their distinctive refusal to stick to set recipes. Wang Xing attributed the “extraordinary efficacy” (奇驗) of Dai Sigong’s cures to his adoption of Danxi’s flexible methods. According to one of Jiang Wusheng’s 蔣武生 (1351-1424) biographers, Jiang “did not hold to the old recipes, but investigated the root of illnesses and made his own recipes; therefore his treatments were always successful” (不執古方而究病所本 自為方 故所治恆十全). $ ^{12} $ The spread of case records would be difficult to understand without the notion that every instance of illness deserved a unique treatment, and without the prestige associated with this flexible style of prescription.

Wang Lifang had allegedly received from Dai himself the cases by Zhu that Wang Xing was prefacing. $ ^{11} $

Wang Lifang had allegedly received from Dai himself the cases by Zhu that Wang Xing was prefacing. $ ^{11} $

collections were actually published in the sixteenth century, and all three of them were the work of men from Huizhou in Anhui. Apart from Wang Ji's book (6.2), I know only of the Categorized Case Files of Illustrious Physicians (Mingyi Leian 名醫類案 [1591], a florilegium of cases by many doctors) $ ^{13} $ and of Sun Yikui's 孫一奎 large collection (6.6), which his disciples collated in the 1590s. $ ^{14} $ Sun was himself a second-generation disciple of Wang Ji. Both Wang and Sun had recorded their cures long before they had planned to publish them, and published them too late in their lives to compete for clients. $ ^{15} $ And since the editors of the Categorized Case Files did not include cases of their own in their compilation, they could not have published this book to attract customers. Since only two men – both from Huizhou and in the same intellectual lineage – published a collection of their own cases in the sixteenth century, no wave of yian was unleashed that would require a sociological explanation. $ ^{16} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Song Lian even claimed that Zhu's "disciples possess books [of these records], classified by illnesses" (門人類證有書). $ ^{8} $

Wang Ji’s two recorded cases of madness implicitly deployed his conceptions of vitality while making full use of the potential of case files for proposing novel arguments. Physicians best displayed their diagnostic prowess when their interpretations diverged from mainstream ones. Wang Ji’s first case was that of a “fat and pale” (肥白) man who had started suffering from a “Heart disorder” (心恙) after he had been humiliated while drunk. $ ^{31} $ The man would “hold a knife [threateningly] or climb walls, screaming with his hair unkempt” (或持刀或逾垣披頭大叫), as typical a case of wild madness as one could find. $ ^{32} $ His pulse (“soft, moderate, and depleted” 濡緩而虛) $ ^{33} $ told Wang that the symptoms were caused by depletion in the Yang-brilliance tract (陽明虛), a remarkable diagnosis, since any educated reader would have known that the Treatise on Cold Injury attributed these

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: In another case by Wang, a woman in her early thirties suddenly spoke mad words and ran around naked with her hair undone, knowing no decency (忽病狂言 披髮裸體不知羞惡). $ ^{43} $ Other healers had adopted aggressive treatments: “Everyone thought it was Heart Wind: some wanted to make her drink fecal juices, others to induce vomiting with anti-mucus drugs” (眾皆以為心風 或欲飲以糞清 或吐以痰藥). $ ^{44} $ Detecting a “submerged, soft, and moderate pulse” (脈浮緩而濡), Wang determined that the disorder was simply caused by hunger and fatigue that had harmed the Stomach (必忍飢或勞倦傷胃而然耳). $ ^{45} $ “Harm to the Stomach has stirred Fire, which has extended to the Heart and Spleen, bringing into disarray the Spirit harbored in the Heart and the Intent harbored in the Spleen because they have lost what they rest upon” (傷胃而火動 延及心脾 則心所藏之神 脾所藏之意 皆為之擾亂 失其所依歸): “how could she not be mad” (安得不

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: As I mentioned in the introduction, calorific replenishing contributed to the emergence of a type of madness (dian) that treatise writers consistently attributed to depletion after the sixteenth century. Calorific replenishing referred to the use of warming drugs to treat depletion Fire (虚火). $ ^{54} $ Warm-replenishing challenged two kinds of therapies: attacking cures and the use of frigorific (i.e., cooling) drugs to “make Fire descend by nourishing Yin” (滋陰降火). $ ^{55} $ Wenbu’s disagreement with aggressive therapies was largely diagnostic. Calorific-replenishers blamed attackers for prescribing coolants in cases of depletion Fire that they had misdiagnosed as repletion Fire. $ ^{56} $ The quarrel between Wenbu advocates and healers who replenished Yin with coolants was more subtle, because both adopted different therapies to treat what they agreed was one imbalance: depletion Fire. $ ^{57} $ Their therapeutic disagreement was itself based on their competing understandings of vitality

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: Most elite physicians from the twelfth century onward, while recognizing that there was “constancy” (chang 常) to illnesses, emphasized that the best healers should also react to “transformations” (bian 變), the events that diverged from the regular ways in which reality was supposed to unfold. “Constancy” itself referred not to unchanging essences but to the regular and predictable way in which processes developed. The emergence of case files as a distinct genre partly stemmed from this flexible conception of illness. Yet even the best English-language histories of Chinese case records usually leap straight from Xu Shuwei to Wang Ji's Stone-Mountain's Medical Case Files (Shishan yian 石山醫案 [1531]; see 6.2) without discussing the dominance of this conception of illness and style of prescription among Jin and Yuan physicians. $ ^{3} $ This omission introduces inaccuracies in accounts of the development of “medical case files” and obscures the process by which they emerged as an independent genre in the sixteenth century. First, despite many claims to the contrary, Wang Ji's work was not the first autonomous work to contain the cases of a single physician. $ ^{4} $ Zhang Congzheng's Ten Configurations and Three Therapies (Shixing sanliao 十形三療), an anthology of Zhang's cases, was edited soon after his death (1228) by his disciples, and was circulating on its own by the fourteenth cen ...

Dai Liang 戴良 and the author of his epitaph Song Lian 宋濂 concurred that Zhu kept detailed records of his clinical encounters. As Dai explained in a text written in 1358 or 1359: For the illnesses [Zhu] treated, what the illness looked like, what excellent remedy he applied, and the drinking of what drugs led to healing [were recorded]. From the past until now, for the efficacious [cases]: who [his patient] was, from what village in what county [he or she came], the names of the interested party, and what [Zhu] had witnessed and heard are clearly documented. $ ^{7} $ 其所治病凡機病之狀何如 施何良方 飲何藥而愈 自前至今 驗者何人 何縣里 主名 得諸見聞 班班可紀 Song Lian even claimed that Zhu's "disciples possess books [of these records], classified by illnesses" (門人類證有書). $ ^{8} $ It is no coincidence that Zhu Zhenheng's disciples and admirers were the leading transmitters of this flexible art of prescription during the early Ming dynasty. Zhu's own case files played a role. Wang Xing's 王行 preface (dated 1377, less than 20 years after Zhu's death) to a compendium of Zhu's cases explained how unusual Zhu's approach had been. $ ^{9} $ Most physicians, Wang claimed, instructed "to treat such-and-such an illness with this remedy, and that such-and-such a remedy treats such-and-such an illness" (治某病以某方以某方治某病而已). But "illnesses change greatly and are without constancy, whereas recipes are fixed and limited" (病多變而無常 方一定而有限). And h ...

“respond to inconstant illnesses with limited recipes”(以有限之方應無常之病)? $ ^{10} $ Wang Xing attributed these singular doctrines (殊不類常聞也) to the aptly styled Wang Lifang 王 立方 (Wang “Establish-a-prescription”), who owed his distinctive ideas to having been taught by Zhu’s pupil Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405; see 5.3). Wang Lifang had allegedly received from Dai himself the cases by Zhu that Wang Xing was prefacing. $ ^{11} $ The success of other practitioners in the “Zhu Danxi” line was also explained by their distinctive refusal to stick to set recipes. Wang Xing attributed the “extraordinary efficacy” (奇驗) of Dai Sigong’s cures to his adoption of Danxi’s flexible methods. According to one of Jiang Wusheng’s 蔣武生 (1351-1424) biographers, Jiang “did not hold to the old recipes, but investigated the root of illnesses and made his own recipes; therefore his treatments were always successful” (不執古方而究病所本 自為方 故所治恆十全). $ ^{12} $ The spread of case records would be difficult to understand without the notion that every instance of illness deserved a unique treatment, and without the prestige associated with this flexible style of prescription. Christopher Cullen’s argument (2001) that case collections started to flourish as a printed genre in the sixteenth century because an increasing number of educated men sought distinctive ways to foster their medical careers after they had failed the civil e ...

collections were actually published in the sixteenth century, and all three of them were the work of men from Huizhou in Anhui. Apart from Wang Ji's book (6.2), I know only of the Categorized Case Files of Illustrious Physicians (Mingyi Leian 名醫類案 [1591], a florilegium of cases by many doctors) $ ^{13} $ and of Sun Yikui's 孫一奎 large collection (6.6), which his disciples collated in the 1590s. $ ^{14} $ Sun was himself a second-generation disciple of Wang Ji. Both Wang and Sun had recorded their cures long before they had planned to publish them, and published them too late in their lives to compete for clients. $ ^{15} $ And since the editors of the Categorized Case Files did not include cases of their own in their compilation, they could not have published this book to attract customers. Since only two men – both from Huizhou and in the same intellectual lineage – published a collection of their own cases in the sixteenth century, no wave of yian was unleashed that would require a sociological explanation. $ ^{16} $ Before we inscribe the “rise of case files” within broad social trends, it may be more prudent to understand the motivations of the individuals who published such works, and to situate their case records in the broader context of their other medical works and of their contemporaries’ use of case files. Stone Mountain’s Medical Case Files was one of as many as eleve ...

which had enjoyed a strong reputation for medical studies since at least the Song, was where the prominent third edition of the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 had been published in 1482 and a sequel called Danxi xinfa fuyu 丹溪心法附語 appeared in 1536. In 1556, Huizhou man Xu Chunfu 徐春甫 completed a compendium called Gujin yitong daquan 古今醫統大全, a mosaic of short texts or excerpts, and had it printed in Suzhou the following year. $ ^{17} $ Two editions of Wu Kun's 吴崑 Yifang kao 醫方考 (with a 1584 preface by Wu) were also printed in 1586, whereas Sun Yikui's works came out in the 1580s and 1590s. These Huizhou physicians were supported by a strong intellectual tradition, a network of active printers, and a growing commercial wealth that facilitated the sponsoring of medical books. $ ^{18} $ If we stop focusing on autonomous case collections, we also realize that Wang Ji was not the only man of his time to record his clinical encounters. Han Mao 韓懋 (fl. 1520s), Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520-30s), Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559), and certainly many others whose names are no longer remembered also did. $ ^{19} $ Although these men did not publish their own collections, their records circulated among specialists. Xue Ji interspersed his editions of earlier texts with cases of his own. $ ^{20} $ The practice of writing case files was thus fairly widespread in the most prosperous region of the empire. Wang Ji's decision to pub ...

shall see in the next section, Wang Ji, just like both his predecessors (Zhu Zhenheng) and his contemporaries (Xue Ji), used his cases to support broader medical arguments. I would say the “case files” genre emerged because an increasing number of doctors kept records of their clinical encounters, both for their patients and for their own reference. Their record-keeping depended on the notion that each bout of illness potentially required a unique therapy. It would thus seem that the sixteenth-century “bureaucratization” of case files that Cullen documented—and the common likening of medical intervention to judicial reasoning and judgment—was simply the repackaging along new metaphorical lines of a kind of record that had become increasingly important since approximately the thirteenth century, when some elite physicians adopted the flexible conception of illness I just mentioned. $ ^{21} $ Charlotte Furth (2007a: 19) has interpreted the case form (in medicine as well as other fields) as a “strategy of modernists in an intellectual tradition that gave awesome weight to the authority of antiquity,” and medical cases as tools to “negotiate the gap between… medical canon and therapeutic strategy.” $ ^{22} $ Without downplaying the freedom allowed by case files to go beyond canonical formulations, I would contend that, at least in the sixteenth century when they came into widesprea ...

### 6.2 Replenishing madness: Wang Ji's case-based reasoning Wang Ji 汪機 (1463-1540), from Qimen 祁門 county in Huizhou, was one of a handful of sixteenth-century physicians who attributed a case of mad behavior to depletion and treated it accordingly. $ ^{23} $ This section outlines the emergence, in the works of Wang and a few more doctors, of a conception of madness caused by depletion that would become more prominent later in the sixteenth century. I first explain the doctrinal background of Wang's predilection for treating patients with ginseng and astragalus root (黄芪), then I present two of his madness cases in light of his broader orientations. Finally I describe the replenishing therapies that other contemporary physicians deployed to treat madness symptoms. Both in his approach to madness and in his general doctrinal discussions, Wang often tackled issues that were discussed in the contemporary medical literature. The larger community of experts of which he was part tackled issues defined by the Danxi corpus (see Chapter 5) and discussed the writings of illustrious authors like Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510; see 5.6). In his work on poxes (Douzhi libian 痘治理辨; preface from 1531), Wang Ji had addressed Wei Zhi's 衛直 recently published Boai xinjian 博愛心鑒 (1525), whereas Wang's work on "external medicine" (Waike lili 外科理例; 1531 preface) integrated arguments he had found in Waike fabui ...

earliest case collections in Chinese medical history, $ ^{25} $ but it also served Wang Ji to make an array of doctrinal points on pathology and human vitality, and to defend his singular clinical approaches. The most distinctive and controversial aspect of Wang's medical practice was his use of calorific drugs (chiefly ginseng and astragalus) to treat cases of depletion that other physicians (following Wang Lun) would have treated with coolants. $ ^{26} $ Wang Lun had attributed visible emissions of Blood to "Fire" resulting from Blood/Yin deficiency, and argued that warming drugs only exacerbated this destructive Fire, and should therefore be proscribed when treating such cases (5.6). Strongly opposing this admonition, Wang Ji tried to show that calorific replenishers could treat not only cases of Qi depletion (as everyone agreed), but also illnesses that were caused by Blood depletion. Wang Ji had to dispute Wang Lun’s authority in his daily medical practice. Not only did rival physicians routinely use coolants to replenish Yin and Blood, it appears that even patients cited Wang Lun’s Miscellaneous Writings of Enlightened Physicians (1502) to challenge Wang Ji’s prescriptions. $ ^{27} $ When he disagreed with a rival’s diagnosis in front of patients and their relatives, Wang Ji sometimes argued that loss of blood, a symptom commonly attributed to Yin/Blood depletion, was in ...

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

“Killing by design” (謀殺), which was understood as the result of prior scheming (謀) between two or more people, was considered the gravest of the six. $ ^{8} $ The law expected such plots to occur in vendettas, fights over property, or schemes to kill one's own spouse. “The one who devised the idea” (造意者) was to be sentenced to decapitation after the Autumn Assizes (斬監後), where it would be classified as “effective” (情實) even if the main plotter had not taken part in the actual killing. $ ^{9} $ The participation of more than one

“Killing by design” (謀殺), which was understood as the result of prior scheming (謀) between two or more people, was considered the gravest of the six. $ ^{8} $ The law expected such plots to occur in vendettas, fights over property, or schemes to kill one's own spouse. “The one who devised the idea” (造意者) was to be sentenced to decapitation after the Autumn Assizes (斬監後), where it would be classified as “effective” (情實) even if the main plotter had not taken part in the actual killing. $ ^{9} $ The participation of more than one

In the three remaining killings, which were all discussed in a single statute, death was understood as being inflicted involuntarily. $ ^{13} $ “Killing in play” (戲殺) occurred in the course of dangerous activities in which people engaged purposely, like sword-practice. $ ^{14} $ This rare crime was punished by “strangulation after the Assizes,” but classified as “suspended” (緩決) at the Assizes, resulting in an automatic commutation to exile at 3,000 li. “Strangulation after the Assizes” was also the basic sentence for “mistakenly killing” (誤殺) a bystander while fighting somebody else. Since this crime was committed during a deliberate act of violence, the sentence was adjusted depending on the gravity of the act during which the lethal

In the three remaining killings, which were all discussed in a single statute, death was understood as being inflicted involuntarily. $ ^{13} $ “Killing in play” (戲殺) occurred in the course of dangerous activities in which people engaged purposely, like sword-practice. $ ^{14} $ This rare crime was punished by “strangulation after the Assizes,” but classified as “suspended” (緩決) at the Assizes, resulting in an automatic commutation to exile at 3,000 li. “Strangulation after the Assizes” was also the basic sentence for “mistakenly killing” (誤殺) a bystander while fighting somebody else. Since this crime was committed during a deliberate act of violence, the sentence was adjusted depending on the gravity of the act during which the lethal
mischance had occurred, or, as commentator Shen Zhiqi 沈之奇 specified in the early eighteenth century, on the intention that motivated the act that led to the mistaken killing. $ ^{15} $

“Accidental killing” (過失殺) could be redeemed by conferring 12.42 ounces of silver to the family of the victim to provide for burial fees. $ ^{16} $ This punishment was mild because the law recognized that the culprit had lacked any will to kill, harm, or even partake in dangerous activities. Since “Killing because of madness” in the early Qing was sentenced in the same way as “accidental killing,” let me cite the entire text of the relevant statute. $ ^{17} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Just as someone who had suffered from madness could be punished severely if his acts were considered intentional, the law also distinguished mad acts from acts committed by simpletons. Like Jia Chandou, Jia Yicai 叚一才, who also lived in the Yongzheng period (1723-1735), had suffered from fits of insanity, during which he would go crazy and run around wildly (病發起來就痴痴癲癲 胡走亂跑了).⁵⁰ When his fits stopped occurring for good, his father released him from confinement (從先原是鎖禁的 後因他不瘋了纔放開了), but by then Jia had become "stupid by nature" (賦性愍獸). He spent most of his time with the children of his small village (有些愍頭愍腦的 好與村裡娃娃們頑耍). An eight-year-old boy often called him a wangba 忘八 or an idiot (你是個獃子), and insulted him by saying that his five-year-old daughter was someone else's (你的女兒是別人養的).⁵¹ Having heard these words, Jia Yicai started suspecting that his wife had engaged in lecherous behavior (淫行). Many times, he cornered the boy and asked him to explain where he had heard this rumor, but the child would not respond.
