rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Killing a senior relative was legally graver than killing an unrelated person. Simply hitting one’s elder brother without inflicting injury deserved 2 years of penal servitude and 90 strokes of the heavy stick. $ ^{53} $ The law on “killing because of madness,” however, purported to apply to all killings regardless of the identity of the victim. $ ^{54} $ If Tingmei was found to have been aware of what he was doing when he had killed his brother, he would be sentenced to immediate decapitation. If unaware (and thus confirmed as mad), he would be handed back to his family for care and surveillance. And because killer and victim were relatives, even a monetary compensation would not be necessary. Killing a senior relative because of madness was probably the only legal situation in which a crime against an older family member was punished less severely than the same crime committed against an unrelated person. $ ^{55} $ Deng was found mad in a first trial and sentenced accordingly, but the Ministry of Punishments wanted to make sure. The staggering gap in punishment between complete exemption and immediate decapitation made officials study Deng’s case thoroughly in a new trial.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: intention was deployed particularly clearly in the network of laws on homicides in which “killing because of madness” would be integrated. The six killings: a hierarchy of intentions Unlike modern Western legal systems, which distinguish between murder and manslaughter or between degrees of homicide, Chinese law in imperial times called all homicides “killings” (sha 殺). Legal commentators customarily divided killings into sub-types. In descending order of gravity, the “Six killings” (六殺) were mou sha 謀殺 (“Killing by design” or “Plotting to kill”), gu sha 故殺 (“Killing intentionally”), dou sha 鬭殺 (“Killing in an affray”), wu sha 誤殺 (“Killing by mistake”), xi sha 戲殺 (“Killing in play”), and guoshi sha 過失殺 (“Killing accidentally”). Three statutes (lǔ 律) sufficed to define killings in the Ming and Qing codes. $ ^{7} $ Here I explain how the “Six killings” applied to the killing of a single person unrelated to the killer. “Killing by design” (謀殺), which was understood as the result of prior scheming (謀) between two or more people, was considered the gravest of the six. $ ^{8} $ The law expected such plots to occur in vendettas, fights over property, or schemes to kill one's own spouse. “The one who devised the idea” (造意者) was to be sentenced to decapitation after the Autumn Assizes (斬監後), where it would be classified as “effective” (情實) even if the main plotter had not taken part in ...

person was what differentiated “killing by design” from “intentional killing” (故殺), which applied when a killer’s homicidal intent was “not known to others” (非人所知). $ ^{10} $ No matter whether this intent to kill was premeditated or arose during a fight (臨時有意欲殺), the lone culprit was judged as having committed “intentional killing” and was sentenced to the same punishment as for “Killing by design”: effective decapitation after the Assizes. “Killing in an affray” (鬪殺) meant killing unintentionally during a fight. $ ^{11} $ In Qing law, intent to kill had become the main difference between “killing in an affray” and “intentional killing.” As a result, those who had killed during a fight had to demonstrate that they had not intended to kill their victim. $ ^{12} $ Under most circumstances, the ensuing sentence of “strangulation after the Assizes” (絞監候) was eventually commuted to the highest sentence below death, namely exile to 3000 li preceded by 100 strokes of the heavy bamboo. In the three remaining killings, which were all discussed in a single statute, death was understood as being inflicted involuntarily. $ ^{13} $ “Killing in play” (戲殺) occurred in the course of dangerous activities in which people engaged purposely, like sword-practice. $ ^{14} $ This rare crime was punished by “strangulation after the Assizes,” but classified as “suspended” (緩決) at the Assizes, resulting ...

mischance had occurred, or, as commentator Shen Zhiqi 沈之奇 specified in the early eighteenth century, on the intention that motivated the act that led to the mistaken killing. $ ^{15} $ “Accidental killing” (過失殺) could be redeemed by conferring 12.42 ounces of silver to the family of the victim to provide for burial fees. $ ^{16} $ This punishment was mild because the law recognized that the culprit had lacked any will to kill, harm, or even partake in dangerous activities. Compensation for a life taken was paid with money, not with one’s own life. Since “Killing because of madness” in the early Qing was sentenced in the same way as “accidental killing,” let me cite the entire text of the relevant statute. $ ^{17} $ Anyone who kills or injures another accidentally $ ^{18} $ (this is comparatively less serious than killing in play) will be sentenced as if for killing or injuring in an affray. [The privilege of] monetary redemption will be granted according to law, and the money will be given to the family (of the person who was killed or injured). (Accident means that which the ear or eye does not extend to, or what was not contemplated. For example, when one is shooting wild animals or when, for a reason, one is tossing bricks or tiles and unexpectedly kills another; or again if, when one is climbing on precipitous [terrain], one's foot slips and one falls, thus involving [i.e., ...

fright, or is driving a cart at a rapid speed on a descent, when the situation makes it so that one cannot stop; $ ^{19} $ or if several persons are carrying a heavy object and the strength [of one] is insufficient to control it so that the other persons carrying it are hurt. In all [these] cases where initially there was no intention to $ barm^{20} $ another, but which led by chance to the killing or injuring of another, sentence as if for killing or wounding in an affray, and grant [the privilege of] monetary redemption according to law. The clause specifying that death could not be foreseen pointed to the difference between accidental killing and “Killing in play.” Accidental death was entirely coincidental.
### 11.2 The first Qing laws on madness: creating a new legal object An early case of parricide The earliest Qing case of madness I have identified was a somewhat enigmatic parricide that took place in 1654, about ten years after the Manchus had seized Beijing and officially named it the capital of the newly established ...

because it was sentenced in a way that differed from (as far as I know) all earlier and later cases of parricide in a state of madness in Chinese imperial history, and because it involved a particular class of people that was unique to the early Qing. $ ^{22} $ Xu San 徐三, son of Xu Jiuwei 徐九緯, was a “hunter” (打牲人) under the supervision of Palace eunuch Shen Maode 沈茂德. “Hunters” (Manchu: butha; Ch.: buteba 布特哈) was the generic name given to several non-Manchu tribes in an area that is now at the confines of Inner Mongolia and Heilongjiang in northern China. $ ^{23} $ One day with a knife Xu San stabbed (戳) his father, who died shortly thereafter, but before he died, the father had time to testify that “his son had been suffering from madness illness” (伊子原有瘋疾). The Ministry of Punishments approved the sentence that an original memorial (now lost) had proposed: Xu San was exempted of any crime (免罪) because he was judged to have killed “because of madness illness” (因瘋疾). This decision had no other legal repercussions because it was not established as a precedent for judging future cases of a similar nature. $ ^{24} $ Since this affair concerned tribes under the direct supervision of Manchu institutions like the Imperial Clan and Imperial Household Departments, the memorial reporting the crime may have been written in Manchu before it was translated into Chinese. As I suggested abov ...

Shunzhi-era (1644-1662) memorials were translations from Manchu. The use of feng bing in these Chinese reports may then have pushed the government to adopt feng bing as the official Chinese term for "madness" instead of the more common feng dian 風癲 and feng kuang 風狂, or the more classical diankuang 癲狂 that had been used in Tang and Song law and was still being used in seventeenth-century medicine. A momentous inaccuracy: the 1667 creation of “killing because of madness” The earliest Qing law concerning madness was a terse rule from 1667 mandating that, “Whenever [people with] madness illness kill or injure another, exempt from sentencing” (凡瘋病殺傷人者免議). $ ^{25} $ In its lack of both context and definition, this rule was typical of the “Currently operative regulations of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingbu xianxing zeli 刑部現行則例) of the Kangxi era (1662-1722), but unlike most Kangxi rules, which usually consisted in precedent-based guidelines for modulating the punishment of existing crimes, the 1667 law created a new penal denomination (罪名): “[people with] madness illness killing another” (瘋病殺人), which I shall render as “killing because of madness.” $ ^{26} $

In imperial Chinese law, a “penal denomination” was not the charge brought against one at the onset of a trial, but the name of the crime for which one was sentenced. Officials selected the fittest “crime name” by assessing the circumstances of the crime, but “[o]nce an offense was judged to fall into a certain category of crime [罪名], the punishment was predetermined.” $ ^{27} $ Both ideally and in practice, the officials who administered this system of fixed punishments were concerned “to maintain a balance between the offense and the penalty.” This judicial approach differed from that of physicians, who gave little importance to denominations in identifying the cause of an illness, and who aimed to put an end to pathological acts rather than find an apt punishment for them. Like the 1654 judgment in the parricide case involving a “hunter,” the 1667 rule on “killing and injuring another because of madness” disregarded the way in which the code dealt with homicides. As we saw above, even those found guilty of “Killing by accident,” the least serious of the six killings, still had to pay compensation to the family of their victim. If the officials who reviewed the 1667 case had been more concerned with the general structure of the code, they should have rejected the verdict of complete exemption. $ ^{28} $ The imbalance in sentencing introduced by the ad hoc 1667 rule was quickl ...

of madness” was integrated into the network of the six killings. $ ^{30} $ From then on, and until the end of the Qing, killings committed in a fit of insanity were legally viewed as lacking both awareness and criminal intention. The 1667 and 1669 laws concerned only homicides, not madness and mad people in general. The 1669 rule in particular integrated “killing because of madness” within the six killings. Note that in both rate and rationale, the compensation the 1669 rule sought for “killing because of madness” differed significantly from the privilege of monetary redemption granted to some disabled people in statute 22 of the Qing code, which was called “Allowing redemption for the old, the young, and the disabled” (老小廢疾收贖). $ ^{31} $ Under statute 22, one could redeem a death sentence with 0.525 ounces of silver, much less that the 12.42 ounces sought for “killing because of madness.” $ ^{32} $ People who were the object of legalized pity under statute 22 could redeem a real sentence with money; whereas in the case of “[people with] madness illness killing another,” the payment was the sentence, imposed for an inadvertent act that had led to a grave outcome. Compassion was not a factor in the 1669 rule, which likened “killing because of madness” to “accidental killing” on the basis of their common lack of criminal intention. $ ^{33} $ For decades, the 1669 law governed the ...

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Most scholars (Chinese and Western alike) who have written on insanity in Chinese history since the 1950s have used the phrase “mental illness” either to describe their object or to pose this notion as the basis for comparing psychiatry with Chinese medicine. $ ^{14} $ But the pure notion of “mental illness” with which Chinese conceptions are so often contrasted (or deemed incomparable) may never have existed. From the era of “mad-doctoring” to the latest edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (or DSM; see APA 1994), behavioral symptoms have been at the center of psychiatric attention.

Since about 1980 in American psychiatry, disorders have only been “mental” at the level of symptoms. The DSM defines disorders by their manifestations, but it assumes that these clusters of symptoms are caused by discrete neuro-chemical processes that will eventually be understood. $ ^{15} $ This folding of mind on brain chemistry is not new: some even claim that psychiatry came of age when it started to trace mental and behavioral disorders to brain pathology in the nineteenth century. $ ^{16} $ Modern psychiatry’s discarding of psycho-dynamics has indeed rendered “the mind” almost irrelevant. $ ^{17} $ So much of psychiatry has focused on the brain (first on its purported “lesions,” now on its chemistry) that we must conclude psychiatric explanations do not depend on a dualism of body and mind.

And for most of imperial history, there were no “types of madness,” only one disorder (kuang or diankuang) characterized by a variety of pathological acts and affects. Whereas nineteenth-century Western psychiatrists referred to different clusters of these symptoms by different names, Chinese texts included many illness scenarios under very few appellations.

The supervenience between mental and physiological processes was inscribed in Chinese medical terminology. Most physicians thought that madness occurred because the Heart (xin 心) or its residing Spirit (shen 神) could not fulfill its functions. rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Kuang illness is made by the deviant [Qi] of Wind when it enters Yang. When the deviant [Qi] of Wind enters Blood, it disturbs the balance of repletion and depletion in a person’s Yin and Yang Qi. If the one is over-replete and the other depleted, Blood and Qi pile on each other. $ ^{32} $ If Qi piles on Yang, one becomes kuang. When kuang flares up, one wants to run and one sees oneself as lofty or worthy, or calls oneself a deity or a sage. Also: the Liver harbors the Cloud souls; sadness and grief stir the center and injure these souls. When the Cloud souls are injured, one becomes kuang, rash, and lacking in lucidity; one dares not face others, the tendons are tense, and one cannot raise the costal bones. If the bodily hair withers and complexion turns bluish, [the sick person] will die in the autumn. All [the above symptoms] are because Blood and Qi are depleted and are subject to Wind deviant [Qi], which makes Yin and Yang Qi pile on each other. This is why [this illness] is called Wind kuang.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: knew no restraints” (性粗疏無拘檢). $ ^{49} $ Liu enjoyed playing the lute (琴), indulged in alcohol, and occasionally wrote texts and songs (時有文詠). $ ^{50} $ The nickname “crazy Liu” probably derived from one common meaning of dian 顛 (“upside-down”), which other sources confirm could be applied to a person’s character. The Book of Liang (636 CE) said of someone that his “heart [mind] was sometimes upside-down” (心有顛倒). $ ^{51} $ One of Yan Shigu’s 彥師古 (581-645) annotations to the Jijiu pian 急就篇 (first century BCE) claimed that “dian illness is when one’s natural dispositions are inverted and abnormal” 顛疾 性理顛倒失常. $ ^{52} $ The History of the Northern Dynasties (659 CE) called a man’s behavior “crazy and foolish” (顛顛癡癡). $ ^{53} $ The graph 癡 may thus have been a merger of 顛 with the old illness name 癡.

It is also during the Song that dian's transformation into a close equivalent of kuang started to draw criticism from physicians. Like Ding Deyong's 丁德用 commentary (1062) on the second-century Canon of Problems, $ ^{66} $ the "Yang commentary" glossed dian 癲 as "falling" (蹶), but it also showed that another usage had become common: $ ^{67} $ "People nowadays take [kuang] to be identical with dian: this is an error." $ ^{68} $ Wang Zhizhong 王執中 (fl. late 12 $ ^{th} $ century) added: "When you analyze dian and kuang illnesses, this is obviously so, yet people never believe it! Could it be that an error in transmission and usage is so difficult to correct?" $ ^{69} $ (其剖析癫狂之病曉然如此而人終不信！豈亦傳習之誤難以改歟？) Even if I found few Song medical sources that glossed dian as a form of madness, Wang's outcry gives the impression that calling madness "dian" was becoming established usage. $ ^{70} $ The next few sections and the next chapter explain how major transformations in Chinese medical thinking altered the way physicians understood and handled mad behavior, regardless of whether they called it kuang or diankuang.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: I noted in Chapter 1 that Martha Li Chiu (1986) scoured the Inner Canon to find a Chinese medical object comparable to mental illness, and that she settled on kuang because it was mostly characterized by "mental symptoms." But by describing symptoms (rather than illnesses) as either mental or physical, she reproduced a problematic dichotomy at a lower level without uncovering the assumptions it hides. Below, I propose an alternative way of comparing Chinese medical approaches with psychiatric ones. My goal is to foster our understanding of medical discourse on kuang by breaking free from the preconceptions that lurk beneath the notions of "mental" and "physical." ### 3.2 Psycho-behavioral pathology in Chinese medicine Comparisons: why not “mental illness”? Most scholars (Chinese and Western alike) who have written on insanity in Chinese history since the 1950s have used the phrase “mental illness” either to describe their object or to pose this notion as the basis for comparing psychiatry with Chinese medicine. $ ^{14} $ But the pure notion of “mental illness” with which Chinese conceptions are so often contrasted (or deemed incomparable) may never have existed. From the era of “mad-doctoring” to the latest edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (or DSM; see APA 1994), behavioral symptoms have been at the center of psychiatric attention. The precon ...

by mental processes (psychodynamics); or they may be considered treatable by action on mental processes (psychological or “moral” healing). Since about 1980 in American psychiatry, disorders have only been “mental” at the level of symptoms. The DSM defines disorders by their manifestations, but it assumes that these clusters of symptoms are caused by discrete neuro-chemical processes that will eventually be understood. $ ^{15} $ This folding of mind on brain chemistry is not new: some even claim that psychiatry came of age when it started to trace mental and behavioral disorders to brain pathology in the nineteenth century. $ ^{16} $ Modern psychiatry’s discarding of psycho-dynamics has indeed rendered “the mind” almost irrelevant. $ ^{17} $ So much of psychiatry has focused on the brain (first on its purported “lesions,” now on its chemistry) that we must conclude psychiatric explanations do not depend on a dualism of body and mind. There are of course differences between psychiatric approaches and Chinese medical understanding of psycho-behavioral pathology, and many reasons to expect Chinese medical objects to differ from psychiatric ones. One of Foucault's most fruitful insights was to show how institutional sites of observation (asylums, clinics, prisons) shaped the objects of disciplines like medicine, psychiatry, and the "human sciences" (2006 [1961], 1989 [1963], 1977 [ ...

and “idiotic” patients were regrouped provided nascent psychiatry with a core of clinical cases on which to hone its conceptual instruments. $ ^{18} $ Chinese medical practices, on the other hand, were not stabilized by institutional boundaries, and most Chinese treatises were textual collages, not studies based on the examination or treatment of a group of segregated patients. And for most of imperial history, there were no “types of madness,” only one disorder (kuang or diankuang) characterized by a variety of pathological acts and affects. Whereas nineteenth-century Western psychiatrists referred to different clusters of these symptoms by different names, Chinese texts included many illness scenarios under very few appellations. Despite the simplicity of this nosology and the marked differences between how psychiatrists and Chinese physicians produced knowledge, kuang and psychiatric illnesses remain comparable, since both have been concerned with psycho-behavioral symptoms. The disorders next to which kuang was discussed (e.g. xian 癎 seizures and dian 癎 “falling sickness”) also resembles the cluster of illnesses that early psychiatry targeted on the basis of the kinds of patients who were confined in asylum. And as we saw above in the section on the Inner Canon, kuang was usually described as being characterized by overt “behavioral dislocation” (Berrios 1985: 166), states ...

honored concern with alterations of social behavior" (Fabrega 1975: 1500). The next section explains how we can understand Chinese medical approaches to this kind of pathology. ## “Supervenience” and its consequences To help make sense of Chinese understandings of psycho-behavioral disorders, let me borrow the concept of “supervenience” from modern philosophy of mind (see Kim 1993; Savellos and Yalcin 1995). For our purposes, we can simplify it as follows: process A is said to “supervene” upon process B when changes in process A (an idea or an emotion, for instance) are always accompanied by changes in process B (say, brain chemistry). Most Chinese medical explanations of madness adopted a strong version of supervenience: not only were manic symptoms manifested in clinically detectable organic processes (excessive inner Fire, depletion of Cardiac Qi, etc.), they were also said to be caused by these processes and they were treated by drugs that acted directly on these processes. The difference with modern psychiatry is that whereas the latter identifies imbalances (in neurotransmitters, for example) that apply almost exclusively to psychiatric disorders, Chinese medicine claimed that Fire could cause any number of symptoms, including many we would consider purely physical (fevers, bleeding, ticklishness, etc.). The supervenience between mental and physiological processes was ins ...

behavioral pathology to an imbalance in fluids, to mental processes, or to both at the same time without dissonance. We find the same double denotation in shen, which could refer to spiritual or vital functions, but also to the fluid that was thought to assure these functions. Common expressions could accentuate either meaning of Heart or Spirit. In “the Spirit is confused” (神昏), shen referred to mental functions, whereas in “the Heart harbors the Spirit” (心藏神), Spirit meant a refined form of Qi (here described in spatial language). Shi xin 失心 is better rendered as “loss of mind” than as “loss of heart,” not only because “loss of heart” implies dejection rather than mindlessness in English, but also because what was “lost” according to the Chinese expression was not the heart as an organ but the functions it was thought to assure. However, when a medical text claimed that “failed Blood flushed the Heart” (败血冲心), the physical organ was unambiguously involved. These two notions of Heart and Spirit provided an established semantic path and a ready-made conceptual schema for tracing psycho-behavioral pathology to physiological processes. They allowed seamless shifts between the mental and the physiological, two domains that English speakers are used to describing with distinct terms even when they do not embrace a mind-body dualism. This being said, both ancient texts and late-impe ...

explained that “in past and present there have been two great disorders: those of the Heart [mind!] and those of the Body” (古今有大病二 其一在心 其一在身). $ ^{21} $ These distinctions between “Heart” and “Spirit” on the one hand and “embodied person” (shen 身) or “physical form” on the other were not used to divide disorders between “mental” and “physical.” All illnesses were grounded in organic imbalances. Diverse illnesses also communicated because they were supposedly treatable with the same remedies. Among the recipes Zhang Lu 張璐 (1617-1699) recommended for kuang in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通), we find a “Dragon-tooth soul-clarifying decoction” (龍齒清魂湯), whose recipe appeared in the section on “tremors” (顫震); the “Angelica Blood-livening decoction” (當歸活血湯), explained in “Blood accumulations” (蓄血); and another brew for which the reader was referred to the headache section. $ ^{22} $ No mental/physical division organized the social organization of therapy: late-imperial physicians were expected to treat all illnesses (except perhaps those of the eyes and skin). We are now ready to explain how kuang and related illnesses were explained in pre-twelfth century treatises and formularies. ### 3.3 Kuang and Wind illnesses The Inner Canon and its derivative works attributed psycho-behavioral disorders to imbalances in the circulation tracts, insufficient Qi, emotional disturbances, or ...

madness is a little-understood process that I will not venture to explain. The evidence for this shift comes from “formularies” (方書), the collections of medical recipes that became an important medical genre after the third century CE. $ ^{23} $ Most extant pre-twelfth century formularies discussed kuang as a Wind illness. But before I turn to these explanations of madness, let me briefly explain the way Wind was thought to produce illness. “The hundred illnesses all start in Wind” In ancient times, Wind was often described (and probably experienced) as a dangerous intruder that could strike humans in their vulnerable spots. According to one common description, Wind was one of six kinds of Qi that were harmful when excessive, the other five being Cold (ban 寒), Summer Heat (shu 署), Dampness (shi 濕), Dryness (zào 燥), and Fire (buo 火). $ ^{24} $ Wind arguably became the most important of the six, as several passages in the Inner Canon called it “the beginning of the hundred illnesses” (百病之始). $ ^{25} $ Not all winds were considered harmful, though. To become injurious, they had to be “hollow” or “empty” (虚), that is, they had to blow from an unexpected direction. $ ^{26} $ Their proper direction was determined by expected cosmic rhythms, including the seasons. According to one influential scheme, a wind was called “empty” because it blew from an “empty palace” (see Yamada 1980 and ...

“deviant” or “stray” (xie 邪), as opposed to “correct” or “straight” (zheng 正). $ ^{27} $ Divine Pivot 75 claimed that “correct Qi is correct Wind. It comes from one direction, neither over-replete nor empty. Deviant Qi is empty Wind that injures people violently. It strikes people deeply and it cannot leave on its own.” (正氣者 正風也 從一方來 非實風 又非虛風也 邪氣者 虛風之賊傷人也 其中人也深 不能自去) $ ^{28} $ Wind was thus an “alien invader” that “harmed by intrusion” (Kuriyama 1999: 251), but not even all harmful winds could cause illness. Only when a deviant wind encountered an internal “void” or “depletion” (xu, the term I translated as “empty” when discussing winds) could it enter the body through the skin (couli 腠理, a term that referred to both the texture of the skin and to its pores), “ride” (乘) on that internal void, and “take residence” (舍) or “settle intrusively” (客) in a specific location. $ ^{29} $ According to the Basic Questions, such pathogenic Qi could settle in individual vessels (pian 56, 63, 74), in organs (37, 74), between organs (39), in acupoints (31), at the top of the head (35), in the skin (56), etc. Tracing the Symptoms of the Illnesses to their Origins (Zhubing yuanbou lun 諸病源候論; hereafter Treatise on Symptoms), which was presented to the Sui throne in 610 CE, was the earliest systematic Chinese presentation of etiology (Despeux and Obringer 1997: 21). Its hundreds of citations in Su ...

unmatched_anchors.source_text: average of 4 cases per century and barely more than 2 if we exclude the last hundred years I cover. I found only two relevant cases between 1588 and 1821. The 35 cases represent the way elite doctors (mostly from the Lower Yangzi region) explained the way they understood and treated mad women. They give only a small glimpse of how society handled women who were called mad. Indeed, the main treatment for manic women may not have been medical. There is too little evidence to tell, but home confinement was certainly an option, though it may have come only after the failure of medical or exorcistic treatments. Since the documents tell us precious little about the way female patients experienced their own condition, we are also deprived of the “first-person” data that have informed recent studies of women in Chinese history. $ ^{2} $ I will remind the reader of these limits when I propose tentative conclusions or try to point to possible trends.

Even one of the best-known Song works on women's illnesses proposed solutions that were equally applicable to men. Chen Ziming's 陳自明 All-Inclusive Excellent Recipes for Women (Furen daquan liangfang 婦人大全良方; hereafter Excellent Recipes), which he compiled in the mid thirteenth century, regrouped a large number of recipes designed to treat disorders as they happened to women. $ ^{6} $ On the issue that concerns us, Chen maintained that, "In women, the disorders dian and kuang are brought about by the deviant [Qi] of Wind, which is absorbed when Qi and Blood are depleted" (夫婦人顛狂病者 由血氣虛受風邪所為也). $ ^{7} $ Despite the mention of Blood depletion, this etiology was not gendered, because it corresponded with the traditional etiology of most Wind disorders. $ ^{8} $

A few sixteenth-century cases tell us what therapies were current at the time. The physicians who designed them did not seem to think these remedies applied to women better than to men. Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520s-30s) traced a young woman's strange behavior (she "would cry or laugh, or run around naked, or shut the doors and speak a lot" (或哭或笑 或裸體而走 或閉戶而多言) to "harm caused by excessive ratiocination, [which made] the spirit unable to hold to its lodging" (思慮過傷 神不守舍也). $ ^{11} $ In the sixteenth century, this etiology typified medicine's increasing focus on "inner harm"—a category that included exhaustion, food scarcity or excesses, and emotional imbalances—and the devising of new ways to treat it. Wu's therapy based on human placenta differed from those proposed in contemporary books, but, as great specialist of medicinal drugs Li Shizhen 李時珍 (1518-

In other words, he was locking one etiology and some symptoms together in a contextually limited script. Li probably believed that the general explanation of insanity as caused by the action of Fire and mucus still applied to women who did not suffer from menorrhagia and whose symptoms did not occur postpartum.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: In both etiology and treatment, extant cases of “mad women” predating 1500 are indistinguishable from those of men. Xu Shuwei 許叔微 (1079-1154) gave the same pill to a woman who had been scared out of her wits by pirates and to a man who had lost his mind as he was about to be executed as a criminal. Wang Haogu 王好古 (13 $ ^{th} $ century) cured a manic woman with a purgative he borrowed from the Treatise on Cold Injury. $ ^{4} $ Two cases by Ni Weide 倪維德 (1303-1377) displayed the same gender neutrality. $ ^{5} $ If these physicians made distinctions between genders, they did not record them in their case files.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Even one of the best-known Song works on women's illnesses proposed solutions that were equally applicable to men. Chen Ziming's 陳自明 All-Inclusive Excellent Recipes for Women (Furen daquan liangfang 婦人大全良方; hereafter Excellent Recipes), which he compiled in the mid thirteenth century, regrouped a large number of recipes designed to treat disorders as they happened to women. $ ^{6} $ On the issue that concerns us, Chen maintained that, "In women, the disorders dian and kuang are brought about by the deviant [Qi] of Wind, which is absorbed when Qi and Blood are depleted" (夫婦人顛狂病者 由血氣虛受風邪所為也). $ ^{7} $ Despite the mention of Blood depletion, this etiology was not gendered, because it corresponded with the traditional etiology of most Wind disorders. $ ^{8} $

In a brief case record, Chen explained that he cured a woman with the “Elixir that nourishes the correct” (養正丹), a common remedy for madness and seizures. $ ^{9} $ But as if to emphasize that this treatment was not designed exclusively for women, Chen mentioned that he also cured a man with the same remedy. Despite the popularity of Chen’s Excellent Recipes after Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559) re-edited them in 1547, there is no sign that Chen’s explanation of women’s mad behavior was ever adopted by elite physicians of the sixteenth century and after, probably because the Wind explanation that Chen had embraced had by then been replaced by a script that traced most problems involving consciousness to the disorderly movements of Fire and mucus in and around the Heart. $ ^{10} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: average of 4 cases per century and barely more than 2 if we exclude the last hundred years I cover. I found only two relevant cases between 1588 and 1821. The 35 cases represent the way elite doctors (mostly from the Lower Yangzi region) explained the way they understood and treated mad women. They give only a small glimpse of how society handled women who were called mad. Indeed, the main treatment for manic women may not have been medical. There is too little evidence to tell, but home confinement was certainly an option, though it may have come only after the failure of medical or exorcistic treatments. Since the documents tell us precious little about the way female patients experienced their own condition, we are also deprived of the “first-person” data that have informed recent studies of women in Chinese history. $ ^{2} $ I will remind the reader of these limits when I propose tentative conclusions or try to point to possible trends. This chapter’s opening section tries to determine whether and how the diagnoses and therapies of “mad women” were gendered. One guiding issue will be whether physicians found relevant the notion that the body of women was “governed by Blood.” The second section focuses on nudity and excessive desires, two symptoms that were likely to provoke extra-medical responses. Perhaps unsurprisingly, we shall see that the gendered character of medical ...

whether postpartum insanity was “really madness,” since late-imperial physicians tied it explicitly to what they called madness. ### 9.1 Non-gendered bodies, gendered etiologies Medical explanations of mad behavior in treatises purported to apply to both men and women. But in practice, did doctors consistently trace cases of women's mad behavior to the roots they identified in their treatises? Did their descriptions and treatments apply to the "androgynous body" (Furth 1999: 25-29, 44-48 and 56-57)—a body without anatomical sex—that characterized elite Chinese medicine? Charlotte Furth has indeed argued that late-imperial physicians focused increasingly less on the uniqueness of women, and tended to understand the women's illnesses in the same way as they understood men's. I shall agree with Furth in general, but I will also show that the etiology of madness was already ungendered in the Song dynasty (960-1279), and that glimpses of gendered etiologies only appeared in late-imperial times, especially in the nineteenth century. In recorded symptoms, identified etiology, and adopted therapy, a spectrum of more or less gendered solutions were available. Some symptoms (notably nudity and grief) were proportionally more common in women's cases, probably because these acts were seen as socially more problematic, and were therefore more likely to be brought to the attention of a physi ...

In both etiology and treatment, extant cases of “mad women” predating 1500 are indistinguishable from those of men. Xu Shuwei 許叔微 (1079-1154) gave the same pill to a woman who had been scared out of her wits by pirates and to a man who had lost his mind as he was about to be executed as a criminal. Wang Haogu 王好古 (13 $ ^{th} $ century) cured a manic woman with a purgative he borrowed from the Treatise on Cold Injury. $ ^{4} $ Two cases by Ni Weide 倪維德 (1303-1377) displayed the same gender neutrality. $ ^{5} $ If these physicians made distinctions between genders, they did not record them in their case files. Even one of the best-known Song works on women's illnesses proposed solutions that were equally applicable to men. Chen Ziming's 陳自明 All-Inclusive Excellent Recipes for Women (Furen daquan liangfang 婦人大全良方; hereafter Excellent Recipes), which he compiled in the mid thirteenth century, regrouped a large number of recipes designed to treat disorders as they happened to women. $ ^{6} $ On the issue that concerns us, Chen maintained that, "In women, the disorders dian and kuang are brought about by the deviant [Qi] of Wind, which is absorbed when Qi and Blood are depleted" (夫婦人顛狂病者 由血氣虛受風邪所為也). $ ^{7} $ Despite the mention of Blood depletion, this etiology was not gendered, because it corresponded with the traditional etiology of most Wind disorders. $ ^{8} $

In a brief case record, Chen explained that he cured a woman with the “Elixir that nourishes the correct” (養正丹), a common remedy for madness and seizures. $ ^{9} $ But as if to emphasize that this treatment was not designed exclusively for women, Chen mentioned that he also cured a man with the same remedy. Despite the popularity of Chen’s Excellent Recipes after Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559) re-edited them in 1547, there is no sign that Chen’s explanation of women’s mad behavior was ever adopted by elite physicians of the sixteenth century and after, probably because the Wind explanation that Chen had embraced had by then been replaced by a script that traced most problems involving consciousness to the disorderly movements of Fire and mucus in and around the Heart. $ ^{10} $ A few sixteenth-century cases tell us what therapies were current at the time. The physicians who designed them did not seem to think these remedies applied to women better than to men. Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520s-30s) traced a young woman's strange behavior (she "would cry or laugh, or run around naked, or shut the doors and speak a lot" (或哭或笑 或裸體而走 或閉戶而多言) to "harm caused by excessive ratiocination, [which made] the spirit unable to hold to its lodging" (思慮過傷 神不守舍也). $ ^{11} $ In the sixteenth century, this etiology typified medicine's increasing focus on "inner harm"—a category that included exhaustion, food scarcity ...

flow out in excess, Blood and Qi confuse the Heart; or when postpartum noxious dew flushes upward and makes speech jumbled and the mind unable to stay steadfast, this is the result of depletion of Blood and exhaustion of Spirit” (有婦人月水崩漏過多 血氣迷心 或產後惡露上沖 而言語錯亂 神志不守者 此血虛神耗也). $ ^{15} $ Li's popular work was among the first to ascribe mad symptoms to pathological menses, but the text explained how one specific imbalance could lead to insanity, not how mad symptoms should be interpreted in general. In other words, he was locking one etiology and some symptoms together in a contextually limited script. Li probably believed that the general explanation of insanity as caused by the action of Fire and mucus still applied to women who did not suffer from menorrhagia and whose symptoms did not occur postpartum. Although the rare sources we have are difficult to bind into a single narrative, dispersed writings indicate that at least some physicians thought that women suffered from madness for reasons particular to them. Zhuang Lüyan's 莊履嚴 Fuke baibian 婦科百辨 (early 17 $ ^{th} $ century?) claimed that the mucus that made women mad (顛狂) by encroaching on their Heart was brought about by extreme fright or sorrow (驚憂之極 痰犯心胸所 致), $ ^{16} $ an emotional theory of mucus that was still relatively rare at the time. In the late seventeenth century, Chen Shiduo 陳士鐸 argued that fits of madness caused by ...

薹憤 遂成此疾). $ ^{17} $ Sorrow appeared in both Zhuang's and Chen's accounts, but neither said much more about it than the lines I just cited. It is tempting to speculate that women isolated from their kin by patrilocal marriage and with limited social relations because of the necessity to stay at home were more likely than men to suffer from “sorrow.” Several cases by Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (fl. 1570s-1610s) indicate that one physician could propose many different explanations for similar behaviors, an aspect of late-imperial medicine with which we are now familiar. In two records published in 1576 and 1587, Gong treated female patients with the “Avert-wind tongsheng powder” 防風通聖散, a formula that a book by his father had claimed could “treat the suddenly erupting symptoms of all madness and Wind disorders” (治一切癲狂風疾 暴發之症).¹⁸ When he re-edited his father’s work, Gong added that this remedy “treats diankuang that has just started to arise” (此方治癲狂初起), when “in most cases there is repletion-Heat and Wind-heteropathy” (多有實熱風邪).¹⁹ In later books, Gong explained that women’s dian behavior (singing at strange times and “climbing on rooftops” 歌唱無時 簫垣上屋) was “caused by constructive Blood (?) lost in the Heart’s Envelope” (營血迷於心包所致也).²⁰ I do not know what Gong Tingxian meant by “constructive Blood,” but it may have been unique to women. As we have seen, however, blockage of the “Heart’s Envelope” ...

imperial times. $ ^{21} $ Gong recommended treating such a disorder with an enhanced version of the “Carefree powder” (加味逍遥散), which was often used to redress Liver imbalances, but he did not record ever using this treatment in a clinical case. I found only one case of a “mad” woman between 1615 and 1822: a file by Suzhou physician Zhang Lu 張璐 (1617-1699). A woman spoke madly, screaming and scolding. She would sing and laugh unusually, as if she were possessed. Her eye and the corner of her mouth on one side were raised in a stupid grin. Some treated her as if these were convulsive fits, others as if these were fits of Heart Wind, all to no avail. It turns out that she had long had a Head-Wind illness, and that it was the acting up of Wind mucus that had made her this way. I used [Sichuan-] lovage-and-wild-ginger decoction with an addition of avert-wind, and she quickly became better after a few doses. $ ^{22} $ 一婦人狂言叫罵 歌笑非常 似崇憑依 一邊眼與口角呆起 或作癎治 成作心風治 皆不效 乃是舊有頭風之疾 風痰作之使然 用芎辛湯加防風 數服頓愈 This way to treat madness symptoms was unusual, but not obviously gendered. The record also indicates that previous physicians had used methods that could have applied to men. Following a disappointing lack of cases for two centuries after 1615, we have ten cases of “mad women” written by five men between 1822 and 1859. $ ^{23} $ Lin Peiqin 林佩琴 (1772-1839) was one of very few doctors who published ex ...

and left several case files to supplement their general explanations. $ ^{24} $ His long survey of causes of insanity included a claim that “women suffer from dian because of Blood imbalances” (婦人患癲 由血不調), $ ^{25} $ but none of his three cases of “mad women” mentioned Blood. He diagnosed that a woman who spoke and laughed to herself was suffering from “abundance of mucus, and Qi in stasis” (痰多氣鬱), and attributed to fright the dian case of a despondent and emaciated (神衰肌削) patient who had been “sitting and lying as if stupid” (坐臥如癡) for more than ten years. $ ^{26} $ Lin noted that “only the massive replenishing of primordial Qi will bring results” (非大補元氣不為功), but since many physicians considered that prolonged disorders led to general depletion, Lin’s approach did not apply solely to women. In another case by Lin, a woman surnamed Zhang was “confused and manic” (恍惚狂妄). In addition to “seeing her husband as an enemy” (视夫若仇), she menacingly “held an instrument and shed her clothes, so that no one dared to approach her” (持械棄衣 莫之敢近). Lin adopted the conventional remedy for such acute crises: an emetic. Pure melon pedicle “removed a lot of viscous saliva and lodged sputum” (去粘涎宿沫頗多), but the violent vomiting caused the patient chest pains. When he saw that she still “spoke and laughed to herself,” Lin “knew that.... the Heart-lodging was [still] depleted and that the Spirit-souls ha ...

unmatched_anchors.source_text: In trials, it was an official's duty to select the “penal denomination” (罪名) that most appropriately described an unlawful act. The sentencing of a crime was called kezui 科罪 or duanzui 斷罪, in which zui referred both to the crime as defined by the Code, and to the penalty that this crime legally demanded. $ ^{6} $ Many legal articles and countless manuals for

or to one that imposed a punishment that was deemed appropriate to the crime at hand. $ ^{10} $ Thanks to analogies, the judicial system could sentence a potentially unlimited number of criminal acts with a limited number of laws. But judicial analogies could only be deployed when no law was found relevant to the punishment of a specific act. This strictly regulated process differed from what doctors did when they treated an illness by analogy to their understanding of another disorder. In Part One, I argued that this kind of “scheme transfer” was the main way in which medical approaches to madness changed.

For almost a hundred years after 1669, homicides committed in a fit of madness were sentenced as “killing because of madness” regardless of the identity and number of the victims, because the law considered that the lack of intention of mad acts justified a light punishment. $ ^{11} $ Starting around the mid-eighteenth century, however, a few concerned officials argued that treating in bulk all killings committed because of madness created an imbalance between some crimes and their punishments. The acts of legislation we are about to examine did not stem from new reflections on madness, but from efforts to reestablish consistent and well-balanced penalties for homicides committed because of madness.

In the second half of the eighteenth century, two important phenomena enabled madness laws to proliferate and become more severe. One was the general proliferation of sub-statutes, which after 1740 could be cited in judgments without reference to statutes. $ ^{12} $ Second was the subsequent development of the sentencing guidelines found in “completed cases” (成案) and in the regulations of the Autumn Assizes. $ ^{13} $ Both events helped to modulate in a formal way the punishments that mad killers incurred depending on whom they had killed.

In the second half of the eighteenth century, two important phenomena enabled madness laws to proliferate and become more severe. One was the general proliferation of sub-statutes, which after 1740 could be cited in judgments without reference to statutes. $ ^{12} $ Second was the subsequent development of the sentencing guidelines found in “completed cases” (成案) and in the regulations of the Autumn Assizes. $ ^{13} $ Both events helped to modulate in a formal way the punishments that mad killers incurred depending on whom they had killed.

Legislative developments stemmed from the initiatives of the emperor, provincial judges and governors, and officials from the Ministry of Punishments. The more highly ranked the proponents of change, the more likely new measures were to be implemented. It was usually the Ministry of Punishments that decided whether to turn such proposals into laws. Eighteenth-century changes may thus have stemmed from the habitus of the Ministry of Punishments and the provincial bureaucracy under the Qianlong reign (1736-1796), a topic we know almost nothing about. $ ^{14} $

The earliest example of a desire to adjust punishments for crimes committed “because of madness” was atypically systematic. In 1754, Shanxi provincial judge Jiang Zhou 蔣洲 (d. 1760) memorialized to “balance the sub-statute on killing because of madness” (請平瘋病殺人之例). $ ^{15} $ He first noted that those who had killed an older brother in a fit of madness had been sentenced in two conflicting ways: 1) to 3 years of penal servitude and 100 strokes of the heavy stick for the “Accidental killing of a superior or senior relative of the second degree” (過失殺期親尊長), $ ^{16} $ or 2) to “immediate decapitation” (斬立決) according to the statute on “killing one’s elder brother,” $ ^{17} $ but with an inserted “slip” 簽 declaring that the killer had been mad and that his sentence should therefore be commuted to “decapitation after the Assizes” (斬監侯). $ ^{18} $ The first kind of judgment reasserted the likening of mad homicides with accidental killings that had founded the 1669 law on “killing because of madness,” whereas the second way of sentencing treated madness only as a

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: The seriousness of crimes was defined comparatively by the main statutes (律) of the Code, which were supposed to form a well-balanced network of crimes and punishments. The Code also provided a table of “mourning degrees” according to which the gravity of crimes against relatives was assessed. $ ^{4} $ As Table 12.1 (next page) illustrates, the order of severity of the six killings was preserved even after the degree of family relation between killer and victim had been factored in. In the table, the punishment increases downwards (toward more serious kinds of homicides) and toward the right (from junior relatives to senior relatives as victims). The middle column defines the standard punishment for the killing of a single unrelated person (凡人). The punishments meted out therefore reflected not only the nature of the act itself, but also the relative status of offender and victim. $ ^{5} $ In the terms of our table, the hierarchy of the punishments imposed for proximate crimes was thus observed both vertically and horizontally.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: From 1786 onwards, the madness-induced homicides of senior relatives were consistently judged in accordance with the relevant statutes, but the death sentence mandated by these laws was diminished from “immediate” to “after the Assizes” because the crime had “not been committed intentionally” (非有心干犯). $ ^{35} $ As this reduction of punishment indicates, the Ministry still recognized that killing relatives in a fit of madness was less serious than killing them while in a normal state. But insanity was now treated only as a secondary circumstance instead of a defining one.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Caizuo 李裁左, who had killed his paternal uncle in 1889, was submitted to torture but he showed no fear (加以刑嚇 罔知畏懼). $ ^{63} $

The second group of mad killers that judicial officials encountered was those who never recovered from madness after committing homicide. Chronic delirium kept a killer from providing the confession that was necessary for a sentence to be pronounced. When such cases occurred, legal reports almost invariably noted that the killer's "eyes were inexpressive and his words incoherent: no confession can be extracted" (两目直瞪 語無論次 不能取供). $ ^{61} $ People like this were considered "permanently crazy" (始終瘋迷). When they were not executed for a serious crime like parricide, they were sentenced to permanent imprisonment.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Given how extremely the Qing judiciary defined madness, it could not attribute crimes like theft to insanity as in contemporaneous England. $ ^{75} $ Theft necessarily implied an intent, a target (money, property), and a purpose (getting enough to eat until the next day, etc.), precisely the kind of details that made Qing officials reject the possibility of madness in homicide cases. In what other circumstances than homicides could the Qing legal system consider madness relevant? What other acts than killings could Qing law consider to have been committed “because of madness”? Here I discuss false accusations and suicides.

It is more difficult to find social roots for transformations in the legal treatment of insane killers. I try to show instead that the dynamics of madness as a legal object was governed by the way the Qing legal system functioned. In the second half of the eighteenth century, two important phenomena enabled madness laws to proliferate and become more severe. One was the general proliferation of sub-statutes, which after 1740 could be cited in judgments without reference to statutes. $ ^{12} $ Second was the subsequent development of the sentencing guidelines found in “completed cases” (成案) and in the regulations of the Autumn Assizes. $ ^{13} $ Both events helped to modulate in a formal way the punishments that mad killers incurred depending on whom they had killed. crimes committed against senior family members. Once some changes had taken place, officials knew that the Ministry would likely approve of more proposals along the same lines, and change therefore followed from change. Since this policy theme did not automatically determine legislation, I explain each increase in punishment individually. As I observed above, this general increase in severity did not stem from a change in the legal conception of mad acts or mad people. For an act to qualify as “mad,” it still had to have been devoid of awareness, and the officials of the Ministry of Punishments still pressed field officials for clarifications when they detected traces of intentions in the actions of a potentially mad killer. The earliest example of a desire to adjust punishments for crimes committed “because of madness” was atypically systematic. In 1754, Shanxi provincial judge Jiang Zhou 蔣洲 (d. 1760) memorialized to “balance the sub-statute on killing because of madness” (請平瘋病殺人之例). $ ^{15} $ He first noted that those who had killed an older brother in a fit of madness had been sentenced in two conflicting ways: 1) to 3 years of penal servitude and 100 strokes of the heavy stick for the “Accidental killing of a superior or senior relative of the second degree” (過失殺期親尊長), $ ^{16} $ or 2) to “immediate decapitation” (斬立決) according to the statute on “killing one’s elder brother ...

secondary circumstance within the homicide of a close senior relative. These sentences were bolstered by two equally valid legal logics, namely the importance of intentions and the weight of seniority in intra-family crimes. Finding these judgments “particularly inconsistent” (殊未劃一), Jiang proposed to calibrate punishment according to the degree of family relation between killer and victim. $ ^{19} $ The killing of distant relatives would be redeemed monetarily according to the statute on killing relatives by accident. $ ^{20} $ One who killed a third- or fourth-degree relative (大小功尊長) whose death usually mandated “immediate decapitation” should be punished by the lower “decapitation after the Assizes.” $ ^{21} $ Finally, the sentence of “immediate decapitation” for killing second-degree seniors should be preserved, but an inserted slip should declare the insanity of the killer. The final decision should be left to the emperor. The Ministry of Punishments rejected Jiang’s proposal, but provincial officials soon had to face again the issues that Jiang had tried to disentangle. In 1756, the governor of Zhejiang confirmed a sentence of “decapitation after the Assizes” for a mad man who had killed his older brother. $ ^{22} $ This was one degree of lower than the penalty of “immediate decapitation” meted out to those who killed an older brother in a regular fight. But the Ministry ...

impose its judgment, but referred the case to the emperor, who entrusted the Nine Chief Ministers (九卿) with the final decision. $ ^{23} $ The congregated Ministers agreed with the governor to postpone death until “after the Assizes.” This way of sentencing madness-induced homicides of second-degree seniors remained in force until the end of the Qing. What made Jiang Zhou, the Ministry of Punishments, and the Zhejiang governor reluctant to treat the killing of seniors as cases of “accidental killing” was probably the view that a killing “because of madness” had an extra component that other “accidental killings” did not possess: direct aggression. Madmen killed with violent gestures. This feature of mad killings may have been what justified the Qianlong emperor's decision in 1761 to make the crime of "killing one's parents because of madness" punishable by dismemberment (凌遲), the harshest form of death penalty allowed by the Code. $ ^{24} $ His pronouncement was part of an edict that instructed provincial officials to execute all parricides swiftly—for fear they would take their own lives or die of starvation in prison—and to report the execution afterwards by palace memorial. $ ^{25} $ The law had long stressed the importance of hierarchical family relations, but in the 1760s the emperor pushed the same logic even further. In non-medical contexts, mad behavior was often understood and handled as if it resulted from possession. When Lin Shiyuan started to “speak and dance madly” (狂言跳舞) in 1762, his mother thought he had been “struck by evil” (中邪); she sent a relative to invite a Taoist master (道師) to chant scriptures and implore deities (誦經祇禳) on his behalf. $ ^{15} $ The Kangxi emperor (r. 1662-1722) was so perplexed by his son Yinren’s reckless behavior that he first attributed it to sorcery and evil spirits. $ ^{16} $ When in 1713 he decided to remove Yinren as heir-apparent after decades of trouble, he had resigned himself that his son’s madness was an illness (瘋狂成疾 and 狂疾) and that Yinren would never recover. $ ^{17} $ The attribution of madness to possession seemed natural in literature as well, as when the son of a corrupt official in a story by Yuan Mei 袁枚 (1716-1797) was said to have “suddenly caught madness” (忽得瘋疾) and it later turned out that the young man was being possessed. $ ^{18} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: In non-medical contexts, mad behavior was often understood and handled as if it resulted from possession. When Lin Shiyuan started to “speak and dance madly” (狂言跳舞) in 1762, his mother thought he had been “struck by evil” (中邪); she sent a relative to invite a Taoist master (道師) to chant scriptures and implore deities (誦經祇禳) on his behalf. $ ^{15} $ The Kangxi emperor (r. 1662-1722) was so perplexed by his son Yinren’s reckless behavior that he first attributed it to sorcery and evil spirits. $ ^{16} $ When in 1713 he decided to remove Yinren as heir-apparent after decades of trouble, he had resigned himself that his son’s madness was an illness (瘋狂成疾 and 狂疾) and that Yinren would never recover. $ ^{17} $ The attribution of madness to possession seemed natural in literature as well, as when the son of a corrupt official in a story by Yuan Mei 袁枚 (1716-1797) was said to have “suddenly caught madness” (忽得瘋疾) and it later turned out that the young man was being possessed. $ ^{18} $

Considering the resemblance between the symptoms of mania and possession, it is not surprising that physicians devised ways to strengthen their interpretive claims on such symptoms. By arguing that disorders that looked as if they were caused by demons could in fact be caused by either mucus or depletion, Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358) managed to discuss possession symptoms without discussing ghosts (4.4). Zhu's argument started its rise to textual dominance in the writings of his disciples and intellectual followers. The sections on "mucus" and "depletion and wasting disorders" (虚損) of Liu Chun's Yuji weiji 玉機微義 (1396) discussed the idea that mucus caused possession-like symptoms. $ ^{19} $ Dai Liang's "Biography of the Old Man of Cinnabar Creek" ("Danxi weng zhuan" 丹溪翁傳), which contained one of the mucus-possession cases I translated in 4.4, was appended to the widely circulated Huizhou version of the Danxi xinfā 丹溪心法 (1482). Wang Ao's 王鏊 Gu danfang 古單方 (completed in 1496) contained the earliest section on "affliction by evil" that I have found. $ ^{20} $ In it, Wang acknowledged Zhu's view that the symptoms of such afflictions could indeed be caused by mucus, and cited Zhu's warning that those afflicted by mucus possession might die if they were treated by exorcism.

Medical texts expressed the similarity between the symptoms of possession and those of medical madness in several ways. Many late-imperial physicians discussed “spectral affliction” under the same treatise entry as insanity. The second half of the madness section in Li Chan’s 李梴 Introduction to Medical Learning (Yixue rumen 醫學入門, 1575), for instance, analyzed the origin of “afflictions by evil” (邪祟). $ ^{12} $ Others doctors proposed explicit similes, as when Zhang Lu 張璐 (1615-1699) noted that one of his insane patients looked “as if he were possessed by a specter” (似祟憑依) or when Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (ca. 1538-1635) claimed that one vaunted recipe “treats fits of madness in which one jumps and screams as though one had incurred a demonic affliction” (治發狂跳叫如著了鬼祟一般者). $ ^{13} $ Li

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: constructive Qi is depleted, one is afflicted by Heat; when one's spiritual Qi is depleted, one is afflicted by ghosts” (夫鬼神猶風寒暑濕之邪耳 衛氣虛則受寒 營氣虛則受熱 神氣虛則受鬼). $ ^{41} $ Xu's explanation rested on the tenet that everything in the world was made of Qi: the sun and the stars, animals and insects, winds, breath, Blood, and so on. $ ^{42} $ In this scheme, “ghostly Qi” (鬼氣) was just another form of environmental Qi that caused illness by invading depleted areas of the body as heat or wind were known to do. Following Michel Strickmann (2002: 24), we could call this ghostly Qi “spectral pathogens.” $ ^{43} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: Zhu Zhenheng's 朱震亨 (1282-1358) influential view that possession symptoms were commonly triggered by mucus. $ ^{7} $ The third section tries to interpret the dramatic decrease in mentions of exorcistic healers in late-imperial cases that physicians or their patients' relatives explicitly attributed to "evil ghosts" or designated as "spectral affliction." I suggest that shamans and elite physicians no longer competed for the same patients. Yet, as I show in the fourth section, even in the late nineteenth century many elite physicians still ascribed illness to the action of various kinds of ghosts. The "medical exorcisms" they performed often borrowed from ritual therapies that today we associate more readily with Daoism than with scholarly medicine. $ ^{8} $ The final section explains how some physicians explained and treated hallucinations, a special type of possession symptom. ### 8.2 The ambiguous closeness of madness and possession symptoms People from the times and places I study often found resemblances between what they called dian or kuang and what they called “spectral affliction” (sui). These categories were not equivalent, but both specialists and commoners found them meaningfully related in a number of contexts. First, acts designated as “mad” could be attributed to possession, because the terms that meant “madness” did not necessarily entail a medical explanation. Ev ...

could treat. $ ^{9} $ Some passages warned that many kinds of spirits could make people crazy. $ ^{10} $ Others called “mad words and blithe speech” kinds of “spectral afflictions” (狂言妄语… 此等之祟) and said that “specters with a form… render people mad” (諸有形之祟…令人 顛狂). $ ^{11} $ If these texts did not assert that madness was always caused by demons, at least they implied that specters could cause some cases of mad behavior. What is crucial is that religious, medical, and legal texts alike could use terms like diankuang to refer a similar set of symptoms. Medical texts expressed the similarity between the symptoms of possession and those of medical madness in several ways. Many late-imperial physicians discussed “spectral affliction” under the same treatise entry as insanity. The second half of the madness section in Li Chan’s 李梴 Introduction to Medical Learning (Yixue rumen 醫學入門, 1575), for instance, analyzed the origin of “afflictions by evil” (邪祟). $ ^{12} $ Others doctors proposed explicit similes, as when Zhang Lu 張璐 (1615-1699) noted that one of his insane patients looked “as if he were possessed by a specter” (似祟憑依) or when Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (ca. 1538-1635) claimed that one vaunted recipe “treats fits of madness in which one jumps and screams as though one had incurred a demonic affliction” (治發狂跳叫如著了鬼祟一般者). $ ^{13} $ Li

Yongcui 李用粹 wrote an entry called “Madness resembles spectral affliction” (癫狂似祟) in his Collected Supplements on Symptoms and Treatments (Zhengzhi buibu 證治匯補; 1687). Another sign of the ambiguous meaning of possession symptoms was that many physicians treated as the result of organic imbalances symptoms that patients attributed to possession. $ ^{14} $ In non-medical contexts, mad behavior was often understood and handled as if it resulted from possession. When Lin Shiyuan started to “speak and dance madly” (狂言跳舞) in 1762, his mother thought he had been “struck by evil” (中邪); she sent a relative to invite a Taoist master (道師) to chant scriptures and implore deities (誦經祇禳) on his behalf. $ ^{15} $ The Kangxi emperor (r. 1662-1722) was so perplexed by his son Yinren’s reckless behavior that he first attributed it to sorcery and evil spirits. $ ^{16} $ When in 1713 he decided to remove Yinren as heir-apparent after decades of trouble, he had resigned himself that his son’s madness was an illness (瘋狂成疾 and 狂疾) and that Yinren would never recover. $ ^{17} $ The attribution of madness to possession seemed natural in literature as well, as when the son of a corrupt official in a story by Yuan Mei 袁枚 (1716-1797) was said to have “suddenly caught madness” (忽得瘋疾) and it later turned out that the young man was being possessed. $ ^{18} $

###### 8.3 Possession... or mucus? Considering the resemblance between the symptoms of mania and possession, it is not surprising that physicians devised ways to strengthen their interpretive claims on such symptoms. By arguing that disorders that looked as if they were caused by demons could in fact be caused by either mucus or depletion, Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358) managed to discuss possession symptoms without discussing ghosts (4.4). Zhu's argument started its rise to textual dominance in the writings of his disciples and intellectual followers. The sections on "mucus" and "depletion and wasting disorders" (虚損) of Liu Chun's Yuji weiji 玉機微義 (1396) discussed the idea that mucus caused possession-like symptoms. $ ^{19} $ Dai Liang's "Biography of the Old Man of Cinnabar Creek" ("Danxi weng zhuan" 丹溪翁傳), which contained one of the mucus-possession cases I translated in 4.4, was appended to the widely circulated Huizhou version of the Danxi xinfā 丹溪心法 (1482). Wang Ao's 王鏊 Gu danfang 古單方 (completed in 1496) contained the earliest section on "affliction by evil" that I have found. $ ^{20} $ In it, Wang acknowledged Zhu's view that the symptoms of such afflictions could indeed be caused by mucus, and cited Zhu's warning that those afflicted by mucus possession might die if they were treated by exorcism. At the beginning of their sections on spectral afflictions, many late-imperia ...

虞搏 Yixue zhengzhuan 醫學正傳 (1515; 5.7), the composite Danxi xiansheng zhifa xinyao 丹溪先生治法心要 (1536; 5.5), Li Chan's Introduction to Medical Learning (1575), Zhang Siwei's 張四維 Yimen mizhi 醫門秘旨 (ca. 1576), Zhang Sanxi's 張三錫 Yixue liuyao 醫學六要 (1585), and most of Gong Tingxian's popular books (7.3) probably enhanced the dominance of Zhu's idea in medical circles. $ ^{21} $ The claim that mucus could create strange disorders had thus became part of mainstream medical discourse by the sixteenth century, and it became such commonsense in the Qing period that it no longer needed attribution. An Anthology of Strange Illnesses (Qizheng bui 奇症匯) compiled in 1786 recounted the case of a man who saw lions in front of him. Without further remarks his illness was attributed to mucus and treated with emetics. $ ^{22} $ About a case of “demonic infestation” (鬼疰) included in his Medical Case Files Ancient and Present, With Commentaries (Guijin yian an 古今醫案按; 1778), Yu Zhen remarked that “This is what is called ‘mucus [causes] many strange symptoms’; it is not something to be marveled at” (此所謂痰多怪證亦不為奇). $ ^{23} $ He commented elsewhere that the commonest treatment for possession-like symptoms in his time was “clearing Heat and guiding mucus” (清熱導痰). $ ^{24} $ Medicine for Sots (Chunzi yi 蠢子醫), a book that one man wrote for his grandchildren in the late nineteenth century, observed twice that the mai ...

disorders was to attack mucus: “when mucus and saliva make a demonic affliction and Cold and Heat go back and forth, the methods of guiding or vomiting [mucus] will be the only ones to work” (痰涎為祟 寒熱往來 非用導吐法不行). $ ^{25} $ ### 8.4 Diminished competition between physicians and shamans? Even if Zhu Zhenheng was never part of an organized campaign to root out popular practices and did not speak in the name of a profession, he clearly aimed to reject exorcism as a treatment for possession symptoms. His understanding of body and illness disqualified shamanic healers to the benefit of people who—like himself—claimed they could reestablish organic balance with herbal or mineral drugs. Though there is no reason to doubt that he was genuinely concerned with his patients' well-being, his interpretation of possession symptoms can also be understood as a move in a competition with other kinds of healers. Ascribing mad symptoms to mucus was a way of saying they were not caused by ghosts and thus of invalidating any recourse to ritual specialists. One of Zhu's cases explicitly mentioned this contest between healers. $ ^{26} $ An adult woman surnamed Jin. During the hot summer months, she went to a banquet. When she returned, her mother-in-law reprimanded her for not sitting at the proper place. She then felt so ashamed that she contracted this illness. In the midst of incoherent words, she of ...

Almost all the Jin and Yuan physicians who recorded madness cases alluded to this rivalry. Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228) explained that the relatives of a man had burned paper talismans (燒符), organized Taoist rituals (作醮), and hired a dancing medium to exorcise the ghost (問鬼跳誣) they thought had crazed him after he had fallen off a horse. $ ^{27} $ Wang Haogu 王好古 (fl. first half of the 13 $ ^{th} $ century) noted that the relatives of a woman who had become crazy “all thought it was a ghost’s making” (人皆以為鬼魔). $ ^{28} $ His younger contemporary Luo Tianyi 羅天益 (ca. 1220-1290) similarly reported that people around his mad patient all said that Wind demons were causing the problem (時人皆言風魔了), though the entreaties of an exorcistic master (巫師) had failed to cure him. $ ^{29} $ Whereas most Jin-Yuan physicians invited to treat mad behavior noted that their patients had previously sought the help of shamanic healers, Ming and Qing doctors almost never mentioned exorcists as their competitors. A complete lack of medical case files between the late fourteenth century and approximately 1515 makes me unable to follow this trend in the documents. All we see is an end result: after 1500, demonology was no longer the most criticized approach to mad symptoms in medical treatises and case collections. Instead, medical writers almost always contrasted their own approach with that of other phys ...

doctors and enough diversity of medical approaches to justify replacing a failed physician with another one, and because these families could afford the multiple treatments. Perhaps the relatives of patients now tended to ascribe mad behavior to organic causes, thus embracing the supervenience of mental events on organic processes that had long characterized learned medicine (3.2). This possible change in belief (and corresponding change in therapeutic choices) is a little understood phenomenon that remains difficult to explain, and even to ascertain. The widespread adoption of the mucus etiology of a large number of disorders may well have extended to people's perceptions of possession symptoms. $ ^{30} $ It could also be that the wealthy and the educated (the main customers of scholarly physicians) had widely embraced Zhu Xi's naturalistic view of ghosts. $ ^{31} $ But these possible transformations in the outlook of patients probably had its counterpart in changes in medical discourse. Instead of denying the idiom of invasion by evil, medicine after Zhu Zhenheng recuperated it. Zhu had proposed a natural explanation of how possession symptoms occurred and suggested an appropriate treatment. As his views spread in medical circles, doctors told readers and patients that visions, odd speech, and unprovoked grief were not always caused by possession. This re-scripting of signs n ...

The fact that protests did not change their neighbors' and relatives' opinions probably vindicated or intensified some people's sense of injustice. $ ^{7} $ Lin Shiyuan's 林時元 statement was vehement: "In the third month of last year [1762], I suffered from some dizziness. They all said I was crazy and they invited a Daoist master to treat me. I am really not crazy at all!" $ ^{8} $ (我舊年三月有些頭暈的病 祇他們都說我瘋癲 叫道士替我醫治 我實在並不瘋) These men were caught in a language game they resented but could not escape, because the words they uttered to dispute their diagnosis only worked to confirm it.

They all said I was crazy and they invited a Daoist master to treat me. I am really not crazy at all!" $ ^{8} $ (我舊年三月有些頭暈的病 祇他們都說我瘋癲 叫道士替我醫治 我實在並不瘋) These men were caught in a language game they resented but could not escape, because the words they uttered to dispute their diagnosis only worked to confirm it.

Yao Zeng 姚增 came to Beijing from Zhejiang in search of the emperor. He “claimed that he could know the heavenly periods and the earthly principles; his words were empty and boastful” (自稱能知天時地理 言語虛誇). $ ^{10} $ In remembrance of his father, Liu Yuhou 劉裕後 wrote a book he called Dajiang pangshu 大江滂書 [Book of the torrential great river?], in which he compared his father to the sages, to immortals, and to Buddhas. $ ^{11} $ Lin Shiyuan called himself “the prime-teacher worthy” (第一師賢). $ ^{12} $

Yao Zeng 姚增 came to Beijing from Zhejiang in search of the emperor. He “claimed that he could know the heavenly periods and the earthly principles; his words were empty and boastful” (自稱能知天時地理 言語虛誇). $ ^{10} $ In remembrance of his father, Liu Yuhou 劉裕後 wrote a book he called Dajiang pangshu 大江滂書 [Book of the torrential great river?], in which he compared his father to the sages, to immortals, and to Buddhas. $ ^{11} $ Lin Shiyuan called himself “the prime-teacher worthy” (第一師賢). $ ^{12} $

Bai Shengqi 白升奇 was born in Xianning 咸寧 (modern Xi'an, Shaanxi) in 1724 and had acquired some literacy when he was young, but soon "abandoned books for commerce" (棄書貿易), said the official report. $ ^{13} $ Since his case is better documented, let me recount at some length what happened to him and what compelled him to seek the help of Qing officials to justify himself. On his way south to Sichuan to sell pottery (販賣瓷器) in 1751, his boat had capsized and he had been greatly frightened. While traveling back home on the Han river, he had lost his belongings and his luggage (將衣物行李遺失). This loss was either the first sign of his madness or the event that triggered it. When Bai returned, his father confined him at home because he thought his son was mad.

The officials in charge first found Yang Xuan, the person to whom the slips were originally destined, but Yang did not know anyone called Li Ⅶ. He only knew two people called Bai:¹⁴ the head of a “community compact” (郷約) and “Bai the madman” (Bai fēngzi 白瘋子), who Yang claimed knew how to write. Governor Zhong Yin 鍾音 sent deputies to arrest Bai and search his house, where they found eleven bamboo strips and five slips of paper with the same handwriting as on the slips that had been delivered to the treasurer’s office. Bai Shengqi was then taken to the Governor’s complex, where, as was customary in cases of possible sedition, he was interrogated by the three highest officials in the province—the governor, the provincial judge, and the provincial treasurer.¹⁵
