page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: ### 14.1 Grievances and reproaches As we have seen, some individuals in Chinese villages were identified as madmen (瘋子) or fools (痴子). More than a simple act of naming, this identification was a diagnosis sustained by the community—close relatives, neighbors, classmates, etc., who probably thought they were simply stating the obvious. "The local community called [Yang Huaizhen] a madman and stopped interacting with him" (鄉黨呼為瘋子 不與往來). $ ^{2} $ Too much is missing from these people's local worlds to understand the difficulties they faced, the way people related to them daily, and the way their treatment differed from the way other poor people were treated. But some themes recur. The commonest grievance I found in mad people's statements was a disagreement with this informal but persistent identification as mad or idiotic. Some people emphasized that this diagnosis came from others. Deng Tingmei 鄧廷梅 (1736): "My son had poxes and died. At once I became so angry that my mind became confused. My ears also became deaf. They said I had gone mad." $ ^{3} $ (出痘死了 一時把心氣成昏迷 耳朵也氣韜了 他們說我瘋了) Ding Wenbin 丁文彬 (1753), whose trial I discuss in 15.4, claimed to have been designated the Son of Heaven by the descendant of Confucius on orders of Shangdi the Lord on High, a title Ding's relatives did not recognize: "[My older brother] is an unreasonable person; he often calls me a fool" (他是不明道理的人 常罵我 ...

were often people who harbored fantasies that their abilities or positions would never allow them to fulfill (as in the expression "the fool who speaks his dream" 痴人說夢). $ ^{5} $ This was the case of "Great King of Shanxi" Lin Zhigong 林志功 (1762): "everybody says I am a fool and nobody believes me" (人人說我是痴子都不信我). $ ^{6} $ The fact that protests did not change their neighbors' and relatives' opinions probably vindicated or intensified some people's sense of injustice. $ ^{7} $ Lin Shiyuan's 林時元 statement was vehement: "In the third month of last year [1762], I suffered from some dizziness. They all said I was crazy and they invited a Daoist master to treat me. I am really not crazy at all!" $ ^{8} $ (我舊年三月有些頭暈的病 祇他們都說我瘋癲 叫道士替我醫治 我實在並不瘋) These men were caught in a language game they resented but could not escape, because the words they uttered to dispute their diagnosis only worked to confirm it. People whom others considered mad often objected to the way their relatives beat them, ignored them, wronged them, locked them up, and (maybe above all else) called them what they insisted they were not: mad. Any rough treatment received under the pretext that they were insane probably intensified their feelings of injustice. Lin Zhigong wrote in a poem that "turning human relations upside-down, the younger brother beats his elder" (顛倒人倫弟打兄). He Cheng'ao's 何成敖 words (1817), recorded after ...

Many people who were identified as mad constructed a superlative persona that made them glorious and excellent. Either before they were socially diagnosed as crazy or as a reaction to this label, they spoke of their dreams and visions and often sought official or imperial recognition for their mission or their virtue. Here I use a few cases to illustrate what a “crazy” persona could be like. Yao Zeng 姚增 came to Beijing from Zhejiang in search of the emperor. He “claimed that he could know the heavenly periods and the earthly principles; his words were empty and boastful” (自稱能知天時地理 言語虛誇). $ ^{10} $ In remembrance of his father, Liu Yuhou 劉裕後 wrote a book he called Dajiang pangshu 大江滂書 [Book of the torrential great river?], in which he compared his father to the sages, to immortals, and to Buddhas. $ ^{11} $ Lin Shiyuan called himself “the prime-teacher worthy” (第一師賢). $ ^{12} $ Bai Shengqi 白升奇 was born in Xianning 咸寧 (modern Xi'an, Shaanxi) in 1724 and had acquired some literacy when he was young, but soon "abandoned books for commerce" (棄書貿易), said the official report. $ ^{13} $ Since his case is better documented, let me recount at some length what happened to him and what compelled him to seek the help of Qing officials to justify himself. On his way south to Sichuan to sell pottery (販賣瓷器) in 1751, his boat had capsized and he had been greatly frightened. While traveling back ...

The officials in charge first found Yang Xuan, the person to whom the slips were originally destined, but Yang did not know anyone called Li Ⅶ. He only knew two people called Bai:¹⁴ the head of a “community compact” (郷約) and “Bai the madman” (Bai fēngzi 白瘋子), who Yang claimed knew how to write. Governor Zhong Yin 鍾音 sent deputies to arrest Bai and search his house, where they found eleven bamboo strips and five slips of paper with the same handwriting as on the slips that had been delivered to the treasurer’s office. Bai Shengqi was then taken to the Governor’s complex, where, as was customary in cases of possible sedition, he was interrogated by the three highest officials in the province—the governor, the provincial judge, and the provincial treasurer.¹⁵ It turned out that Bai's motives for presenting the slips were far from those of a rebel. Asked what his intentions were (究屬何心), he answered as follows: I lost my clothes and things in the Han River and no one pursued [this matter]. I have also been locked up for many years, which has been ghastly and difficult to bear. I wrote the names of people I know on bamboo strips and paper slips, and filled out the official posts, hoping to make them look for the lost objects for me. Because I do not see many people from year to year, I had [the slips] sent to the provincial treasurer's yamen, so that he could help me search the thing ...

to emperorship or if the self-aggrandizing “fifth year of Liqi” only sounded comforting in distressing times. $ ^{16} $ Zhang Tingyong 張廷用 also managed to sound politically subversive even as he constructed his "delirium" from daily objects and local names of people and organizations. $ ^{17} $ Of course his words were also designed to sound politically meaningful. As a youth, "Zhang Tingyong had participated in a [semi-annual local] examination to become a licentiate but had not been admitted. $ ^{18} $ This turned into madness/idiocy: he would constantly chant the Buddha's name; [the illness was] intermittent" (張廷用曾考試式童未能進取 染成瘋癡隨口念佛 時發時止). In 1748, having shaved his hair and queue (自剃髮辦), he went to Wutai Mountain to become a monk, but the bonzes in charge (當處僧人) made him return home. In 1756, when Zhang's illness became more serious, his family declared him to the county office through the head of the local community compact (經鄉約報官). The county issued chains and locks and delivered Zhang to his relatives, who were instructed to lock him up and keep watch over him (發給鎖封 交伊親屬鎖錮看守). $ ^{19} $ In 1760, he twisted his locks open and managed to flee to the city (扭鎖脫身 奔入城市), where he was captured by the magistrate's men. He was sent back home with new locks (仍發鎖封看守). The county government had files on both events (各有案). In 1767, while everybody was working in the fields, Zhang brok ...

manuscript notes that he may have written over the years or along the road (the report did not tell). He was caught again. As the magistrate in charge of his case reported: The text is mediocre and pedestrian, preposterous and confused, constantly redundant and not forming full sentences. But among the words we find "White-rain dragon-king association," "Society of the Ten Buddhas," and the names of people in those associations, such as "Liu Guangrong," "Association Leader Cao," and "Association Leader Wang." I am very afraid that [these names] hide something else, such as [sutra?] reading associations. 閱其鄙俚誕妄迷係重複雷同 不成語句 惟字內載有白雨龍王會、十佛會及會中人劉光榮 曹會長 王會長名 甚恐有念□會別情 Symbolically loaded language made officials apprehensive. Zhang Tingyong's slips seemed to mention the kind of religious association that was often banned under the label of “devious cult” (邪教). Such religious groups were common in Qing China, but the state feared they might develop into large-scale organizations that could rival the state in their sway over “people’s hearts.” $ ^{20} $ The officials who interrogated the local leaders and the people Zhang Tingyong named in his notes soon found that there was no rebellion in the making. The “Society of the Ten Buddhas” was the name of a long-banned association that was named after ten days representing the birthdays of ten gods and Buddhas (神佛誕日 十項相傳). The report specified ...

who sought his services and chose auspicious days for marriages and funerals (代人酹神祈禱 并選擇婚葬日期), but practiced no occult arts. The lofty-sounding “dragon-king association” turned out to be an agricultural cult and “white rain” the name local peasants gave to hailstones (冰雹). From the fourth to the eighth lunar month, the villagers went to the neighboring temple of the dragon spirit.... to burn incense and paper to [?] and pray (村農至附近龍神廟內□帶香褚靼流祈禱) that the hail would not harm the crops: “there are in fact no such things as gatherings of crowds to read sutras or the establishment of devious cults” (實無聚眾念經、創立邪教情事). The practice of presenting incense at the local dragon-god temple was in fact consistent with what the Code called “charitable societies that conduct prayers in the spring and fall” (在本村神龍廟靼月進香 與律載春秋義社以行祈报者相同 似無庸置議). $ ^{21} $ The Governor of Shanxi clarified small details: under interrogation in Zhili soon after he was caught, Zhang Tingyong had mentioned the existence of “one effortless knife kept by xianzi [i.e., a sage]” (顺刀一把是賢子收著). The “knife kept by the sage” was not the fantastic weapon or religious object that officials feared it might be. The Shanxi Governor, who had a copy of Zhang’s statement, discovered that Zhang’s house was near a mountain and that the family owned an old knife for protection against wild animals, which was kept in the house of “this crimin ...

Because Zhang had not impinged on the sanctity of imperial symbols, he was not physically punished. As the 1767 report concluded, “it has been established that this criminal Zhang Tingyong is indeed mad; according to the [1762] sub-statute [on those who have committed a killing because of madness], the Yingzhou magistrate shall be ordered to confine him and take the proper precautions, so that [Zhang] will not be allowed to break out and stir up trouble again” (该犯张廷用實屬瘋癲 應飭令應州知州照例嚴加鎖禁防範 毋使再以出境滋事). We know of Bai Shengqi and Zhang Tingyong through third-person reports on their words and acts. We now turn to someone who left his own writings, and whose view on life we therefore know much more about than Bai’s and Zhang’s. ### 14.3 “Idle steed” Lin Zhigong and his writings Lin Zhigong's 林志功 case is one of the most interesting to have survived in the records. $ ^{22} $ Lin was born in 1708 in Changshan 常山 county in Quzhou 衢州, the westernmost prefecture of Zhejiang, near the border of Jiangxi province. He became mad with grief after his wife and one of his two sons died of disease early in 1735. His relatives tried to have him exorcized, but without success. A new marriage was nonetheless arranged between Lin and a young bride from Yushan 玉山, a county west of Changshan in Jiangxi. At an unspecified time, Lin's relatives declared him to the county government and confined him at home, ...

unmatched_anchors.source_text: the reports. Interestingly, all made their way into depositions. $ ^{8} $ Bureaucratic jargon is particularly important because it appeared everywhere. Only good knowledge of legal texts and the reading of numerous tiben allow to separate the formulaic from words that the witnesses would have been more likely to recognize as their own. Before I turn to the depositions themselves, let me survey the main set phrases that punctuated legal reports and how we can use them to some profit despite their seemingly opaque uniformity.

This being said, the language of reports suggests that madness could be discerned in one’s facial expression or countenance (神色). The visage of many mad men was described as “confused” (神色昏迷). $ ^{11} $ By contrast, the aspect and language of a recovered person were said to be “sober” or “clear” (清爽). $ ^{12} $ A longer set phrase specified that the recovered killer’s “facial expression was sober and his speech clear” (神清色爽 語言明晰). $ ^{13} $ Since officials knew that legal madness was all or nothing: the reports they wrote therefore made as strong a distinction as possible between the normal and the mad: a dumb-struck expression contrasted with a sober one; incoherent speech with clear language.

Tiben often cast the events that had led to a killing into the predetermined script of how the law expected madness cases to unfold. Because most mad killings were committed during sudden fits and because the law defined madness as utter unawareness of one's own actions, the vast majority of memorials specified that on such-and-such a date, the would-be killer "got madness illness, with intermittent fits; when it flares up, he is unaware of people and things" (得了瘋病 時發時止 發時不醒人事). $ ^{14} $ This was how the prototypical scenario of a mad homicide started.

Medical case files also contained formulaic turns of phrase, but they tended to be more flexible, perhaps because they recorded symptoms that physicians had observed (by contrast with both writers of medical treatises, who were trying to present general arguments, and legal officials or secretaries, who had not witnessed the acts of the people they were describing) and because these symptoms could be important to the establishment of a therapy. $ ^{16} $ The readers of medical case files also differed from those of legal reports. Whereas legal reports were meant to be assessed by superiors or equals, medical records were written for the relatives of patients or for readers of collections of case files. Although physicians occasionally used formulaic phrases (often descriptions of symptoms drawn from the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon) in their case files, they had nothing to gain from channeling all their cases into the same convenient mold when the uniqueness of each treatment was precisely what brought them distinction.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: In some madness cases, all the witnesses were cited as having used the same words in the same order to describe what happened on the day of a crime. Even the killer used the same words despite claiming that he had no memory of what had happened. Most people who had not declared a mad person to the authorities explained their decision by claiming that their mad relative or neighbor "was not inciting trouble" (並不滋事). $ ^{6} $ However, when a father explained that he did not declare his son "because he is my only son," we may presume that the report was not citing a legal secretary playing ventriloquist. The language itself may not have been authentic, but the content probably was.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: The magistrate composed a summary of the entire affair and proposed a temporary sentence according to the relevant laws. He sent his report to his immediate superior the prefect, along with the formal depositions (招狀) of the criminal and of all the witnesses, reconstituted from the clerk's notes. I have already discussed the several reviews this preliminary sentencing had to go through before it could be confirmed (see 10). The depositions we read in these provincial reports were thus several times removed from the words that the parties had originally uttered. Yasuhiko Karasawa (2007) has noticed broad discrepancies between draft depositions and the way they were cited in later reports. $ ^{5} $ He has shown that during the course of the Qing the legal secretaries who composed the final drafts of tiben increasingly tended to edit testimonies so as to confirm the magistrate’s (and the governor’s) findings, removing from the depositions lies, smokescreens, gaps or contradictions in evidence, and all the possibly truthful details that did not appear in the official narrative of the case (which in turn purported to be based on the confessions). In some madness cases, all the witnesses were cited as having used the same words in the same order to describe what happened on the day of a crime. Even the killer used the same words despite claiming that he had no memory of what had happ ...

Depositions were edited translations of orality rather than direct transcriptions. Conventions of legal reporting such as the use of the written style and the selective editing of depositions make it difficult to assess what terms common people used to describe madness. Very specific depositions and those that “sound oral” were probably closer to the original words of the witnesses than the conventionally formulated ones. The memorials that quoted the witnesses as having said different things with different words in two different trial sessions are also more likely to have been authentic. The fact that these depositions consistently sounded more oral seems to confirm this view. It remains possible, however, that in many cases some seemingly oral usage reflected a secretary’s use of a vernacular style of writing as a device to enhance the authenticity of the depositions. Even when depositions recorded people’s voices accurately, however, they did not necessarily reflect what commoners would have said about the people under trial if they had been interacting with them or talking about them in a daily context. Since we have no access to the words that the witnesses actually pronounced, the best we can do is to assess each memorial on an individual basis while keeping in mind all the caveats I have just outlined. $ ^{7} $ ## The interest of the formulaic The expressions concerning ...

the reports. Interestingly, all made their way into depositions. $ ^{8} $ Bureaucratic jargon is particularly important because it appeared everywhere. Only good knowledge of legal texts and the reading of numerous tiben allow to separate the formulaic from words that the witnesses would have been more likely to recognize as their own. Before I turn to the depositions themselves, let me survey the main set phrases that punctuated legal reports and how we can use them to some profit despite their seemingly opaque uniformity. The concepts we see in documents were more than “background noise” that should be reduced before we can get to the undistorted facts. These objects were precisely those that institutions and practitioners purported to discuss and act upon. Formulaic phrases may have given little idea of what the person they described looked and sounded like, but they spoke eloquently of what the legal field expected madness to sound and look like. Judicial reports almost invariably noted that the eyes of killers who had just been arrested “were staring straight” (兩目直瞪 or 兩目直視) and that their “words were incoherent” (語無論次). These words described the stereotypically stupefied-and-rambling madman who could not provide the deposition that was necessary for sentencing to take place. Since these reports were written only after painstaking investigations had confirmed that the pers ...

recognized by these outward signs would be putting the result (an ex post facto report phrased in unequivocal terms) before the process of identification. This being said, the language of reports suggests that madness could be discerned in one’s facial expression or countenance (神色). The visage of many mad men was described as “confused” (神色昏迷). $ ^{11} $ By contrast, the aspect and language of a recovered person were said to be “sober” or “clear” (清爽). $ ^{12} $ A longer set phrase specified that the recovered killer’s “facial expression was sober and his speech clear” (神清色爽 語言明晰). $ ^{13} $ Since officials knew that legal madness was all or nothing: the reports they wrote therefore made as strong a distinction as possible between the normal and the mad: a dumb-struck expression contrasted with a sober one; incoherent speech with clear language. Tiben often cast the events that had led to a killing into the predetermined script of how the law expected madness cases to unfold. Because most mad killings were committed during sudden fits and because the law defined madness as utter unawareness of one's own actions, the vast majority of memorials specified that on such-and-such a date, the would-be killer "got madness illness, with intermittent fits; when it flares up, he is unaware of people and things" (得了瘋病 時發時止 發時不醒人事). $ ^{14} $ This was how the prototypical scenario of a mad ...

Since only absolute insanity was legally recognized, it is possible that many people whom trials determined were mad probably looked the way set phrases described them. But these formulaic expressions were meant to attest more than to describe. By establishing a seamless correspondence between specific events and a general scenario, officials used these phrases to make the choice of the penal denomination “killing because of madness” appear as indisputable as possible. This “fool-proofing” of judicial sentences was important, because officials could be imposed administrative sanctions if they sentenced a crime too lightly, too heavily, or according to the wrong legal article. Concrete observations funneled into a few turns of phrase aimed to convince reviewers that the madness of the “criminal” under trial was indeed as extreme as the law mandated. $ ^{15} $ The criminal's recovery from his incoherence made it possible to extract a confession. In most reports from the late Qing, when testimonies were rewritten to confirm the final verdict, few words were the criminal's own. The killer and most of the witnesses were reported to have said about the same thing in about the same words. The killer insisted that he "did not know how" (不知怎樣), he had grabbed an object – a club, a vegetable knife (菜刀, very common), a carrying pole, a staff, an axe, a sickle, a stool, a cane, a knife to ...

The confession was not an admission of guilt doubled with repentance (though depositions sometimes contained statements of regret, dismay, or contrition), but the killer's formulation of the crime for which he would be sentenced—his act as it was characterized by the law. Since by definition he did not remember his acts, his confession could only be the ratification of the version of the crime that officials had settled upon after investigation. Medical case files also contained formulaic turns of phrase, but they tended to be more flexible, perhaps because they recorded symptoms that physicians had observed (by contrast with both writers of medical treatises, who were trying to present general arguments, and legal officials or secretaries, who had not witnessed the acts of the people they were describing) and because these symptoms could be important to the establishment of a therapy. $ ^{16} $ The readers of medical case files also differed from those of legal reports. Whereas legal reports were meant to be assessed by superiors or equals, medical records were written for the relatives of patients or for readers of collections of case files. Although physicians occasionally used formulaic phrases (often descriptions of symptoms drawn from the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon) in their case files, they had nothing to gain from channeling all their cases into the same convenient mo ...

affliction by nine different terms. $ ^{17} $ Putting aside those related to “mucus” for the moment, they were “madness illness” (the official term for madness), fengbing 風病 (lit. “Wind illness,” but homophonous with the term that meant “madness illness”), fengdian 風顛 (“madness”; lit. Wind dian), $ ^{18} $ fengkuang 風狂 (“madness”; lit. Wind kuang), diankuang 顛狂 (“insanity”), $ ^{19} $ fengkuang bing 風狂病 (“madness illness”), fengkuang bingzheng 風狂病症 (“madness disorder”), and fengdian bingzheng 風顛病症 (“madness disorder”). $ ^{20} $ We cannot assume that people used the term “madness illness” orally. Considering that fengdian and fengkuang were intelligible on their own and were widely used in non-judicial contexts, we should probably interpret the compounds fengkuang bing, fengkuang bingzheng, and fengdian bingzheng—which were clearly not oral—as bureaucratic jargon. The suffixes bing and bingzheng may have been used to make clear that the law considered madness to be an illness. $ ^{21} $ Apart from the pair-turned-compound diankuang, all the above terms contain the sound feng. A clerk or official who wanted to transcribe the sound feng could choose between the homophonous 瘋 and 風. The fact that the report on Deng Tingmei’s case used 瘋 only when quoting the letter of the law suggests that the 瘋 usage was still marginal at the time. $ ^{22} $ The last two “terms for madness” emplo ...

(“Wind-mucus blocking the openings [of the Heart]”). In accord with medical conceptions I have already analyzed, tanmi xinqiao identified mucus as the cause of madness and the Heart as the seat of cognitive and perceptive activities. $ ^{23} $ “Wind-mucus blocking the openings” was a variant specifying that mucus had been caused by Wind. $ ^{24} $ This Wind etiology, which had become exceptional in Qing medicine, was reflected in the way the report explained the onset of Deng’s madness. Whereas most memorials said that the would-be killer “got madness” (得了瘋病), the 1736 memorial claimed Deng “contracted” (run 染) it, a term usually reserved for illnesses caught by contact with some kind of pathogenic Qi. $ ^{25} $ Although not all these terms for madness were technical medical categories, most of them had equivalents in the medical literature. The use of medically acceptable terminology by commoners and legal specialists alike does not imply that these people embraced medical conceptions. It confirms instead that the same set of terms could accommodate many different conceptions. The non-technical medical words used in legal depositions differed from expressions like “Liver stasis” (肝鬱), which referred to a specifically medical understanding of pathology rather than to a general kind of behavior like fengkuang. $ ^{26} $ In the same 1736 memorial, several people described the ons ...

Wang Qi 王琦 made enormous efforts to find an official who would accept his brother Xun's 珣 text until an illiterate military official agreed to receive it. $ ^{7} $ In the rest of this chapter, I examine the writing practices of men who were not classically literate but shared dreams of achieving something through writing. Belief in the power of writing can be observed both in their naive hope that a text would bring rewards when presented to imperial administrators, and in the often frenzied reaction of officials to these text-bearers.

As many of the men I discuss in this chapter, Chai Shilu's brother Shijin 世進 came to official attention while trying to present a text to an imperial administrator. Donations of letters and texts were not always illegal, but the Qing code contained a law that forbade the presentation of texts in order to gain recognition or employment: seeking advancement under a false pretense was to be punished with 100 strokes of the heavy stick. $ ^{9} $

Despite the heavy punishment incurred, all sorts of people presented texts to officials to attract their attention. Many of the cases I discuss in this chapter commenced in a similar fashion, when “mad people” contacted the officialdom to voice grievances, to express praise for the emperor, or to seek advice or employment. $ ^{10} $ Education commissioners’ (學證) constant travels, their proximity to licentiates, and their moral reputation made them favorite targets for grievance bearers and letter writers. $ ^{11} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: In any case, as we saw in the case of Liang Nianquan, the fact that someone was recognized as crazy did not make his words any less seditious. Da ni (“sedition” or “great defiance”), the crime for which Ding was executed, was one of the “Ten abominations” (十惡). Ni did not mean putting together a full-scale rebellion; $ ^{51} $ it meant “going against,” with the additional sense of going in the wrong direction, against the flow of a river, for instance. Ding’s writings were transgressive not in the sense of having “gone too far,” but of having “gone against.” He “went against” because he played on the same symbolic field as the emperor. Ding was not seen as an inoffensive parody of imperial symbolic prestige, because he challenged the reigning dynasty by recuperating its main symbols of legitimacy. But these “public symbols” were for public view, not for public use.

A flurry of articles on that subject appeared in the first year of publication of several academic or semi-academic journals in 1979 and 1980, soon after the Third Plenum of the Eleventh Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party in late 1978 had confirmed the fall of the Gang of Four and modified conditions of scholarship. $ ^{5} $ An even more recent wave of scholarship on wenziyu has by and large retained the view that the Qing indiscriminately repressed young Chinese thinkers and intellectuals. $ ^{6} $ Anecdotal accounts and emphasis on the unfairness of the condemnations are still the chief aspects of this scholarship. Now that the anti-Manchu sentiment has disappeared (since the Manchus are now retrospectively presented as a “minority nationality” within the broad Chinese nation), the fault for wenziyu is rejected on “feudal government” in general. There may be issues that partisan debates and anecdotal accounts have kept us from noticing in these trials. What kinds of words or writings were considered seditious enough to deserve death even as those who uttered them were recognized as crazy? What type of political regime could feel threatened by such words? In what contexts were expressions of loyalty unacceptable? Are we being misled by what we feel is a clear boundary between madness and crime? I seek answers to these questions in four interrelated domains ...

his friends and shop-keeping neighbors in the 1740s and 1750s until he came to the attention of the government. Wang Qi 王琦 made enormous efforts to find an official who would accept his brother Xun's 珣 text until an illiterate military official agreed to receive it. $ ^{7} $ In the rest of this chapter, I examine the writing practices of men who were not classically literate but shared dreams of achieving something through writing. Belief in the power of writing can be observed both in their naive hope that a text would bring rewards when presented to imperial administrators, and in the often frenzied reaction of officials to these text-bearers. Presenting letters and petitions (tou shu 投書, tou ci 投詞) This truly is something he did because his madness flared up. It he had been lucid, would he have dared to burst into a yamen carrying a rebellious note, throwing himself to his death? 這實是他因瘋發做出來的事 若是明白 還敢執持逆帖 闖入衙門投死嗎 Chai Shilu 柴世祿, 1762 As many of the men I discuss in this chapter, Chai Shilu's brother Shijin 世進 came to official attention while trying to present a text to an imperial administrator. Donations of letters and texts were not always illegal, but the Qing code contained a law that forbade the presentation of texts in order to gain recognition or employment: seeking advancement under a false pretense was to be punished with 100 strokes of the heavy stick. $ ^{9} $

Despite the heavy punishment incurred, all sorts of people presented texts to officials to attract their attention. Many of the cases I discuss in this chapter commenced in a similar fashion, when “mad people” contacted the officialdom to voice grievances, to express praise for the emperor, or to seek advice or employment. $ ^{10} $ Education commissioners’ (學證) constant travels, their proximity to licentiates, and their moral reputation made them favorite targets for grievance bearers and letter writers. $ ^{11} $ Offerings of dubious texts were matters serious enough to be discussed in palace memorials, a form of document designed for reporting on important state matters. Governors and Governors-general usually conducted interrogations in person and were responsible for mobilizing lower officials for swift and thorough investigations. Officials scrutinized the family background and network of relations of the “criminals,” their possible involvement in unlawful activities, and whether they were concealing more “illicit writings.” $ ^{12} $ Official suspicion about these texts contrasted with the hopes of those who presented them. Wang Zhaoji 王肇基 presented a poem in praise of the emperor to a Shanxi prefect in 1751, but his eulogy was found to include "recklessly seditious passages" (狂妄悖逆之處) and words that "slandered the sages" (毀謗聖賢). $ ^{13} $ As he confessed, "In offering a ...

Majesty”(我獻詩恭祝皇太后萬歲 不過盡我小民之心 欲求皇上喜歡的意思). He was sentenced to death by beating, a heavy punishment considering that officials admitted he did it “without awareness”(無知).¹⁴ The use of this irregular punishment indicates that Wang’s “crime” was considered serious enough to justify disregard for penal denominations, but that it was not completely on a par with treason or sedition, which would have led to the execution of all of Wang’s male relatives and the enslavement of his female kin. For officials who were concerned with sedition (or advancement), one issue was how to prevent commoners from presenting “seditious petitions.” Chen Hongmou 陳宏謀 (1696-1771), activist official par excellence, noted in a 1763 memorial that non-violent madmen were rarely confined, because they were unlikely to commit a homicide. $ ^{15} $ Expressing his fear that some men would exploit this “weakness” of the policy of preventive confinement to foster disorder, Chen proposed to imprison as strictly as violent madmen those insane people who were meek but literate. They should also be denied access to writing implements. We know of Chen’s memorial through an appreciative edict by the Qianlong emperor: $ ^{16} $ There is some vision in what is reported in Chen Hongmou's memorial requesting the locking up of mad people. She had indeed accompanied the emperor on his fourth Southern Tour in early 1765, but for reasons that are still unclear, the monarch suddenly had ordered her to return to Beijing. She either died of grief or committed suicide in the palace the following year, after she had obviously fallen out of favor. $ ^{22} $ Yan presented his message to a Shanxi official in 1777. Written in the form of a palace memorial, it was a sympathetic reproof of the sovereign's alleged misconduct: Empress Na[la] was plain and excellent, principled and chaste, and had been blessed with much doting. Seeing that His Majesty, despite being over five-decades old and busy with numerous and complicated matters of state, still showed special favor to ladies as in the past, she was afraid this was not a good way of nurturing the Sagely imperial body. Some of the doubts that officials expressed about the madness (or “besottedness”) of text bearers probably resulted from bureaucratic necessity. When reporting on a case, they could not assume that one who looked or sounded crazy was crazy. This “institutionalized strategy” (Bourdieu 1990: 138) on the part of officials was the object of no specific rule, but it was strongly encouraged in bureaucratic practice. Those officials who embodied this fear of mad-sounding speech were praised, whereas officials who simply assumed that the authors of these texts were mad were punished if their negligence was later discovered. $ ^{25} $ But even when officials had found no illegal activities behind a suspicious text, they were not necessarily clement. Although Feng Qiyan was eventually recognized to have suffered from “mucus confusion,” his punishment was not lenient. Feng Qiyan’s having illicitly written a petition for a trifling marriage matter and intended to present it in front of the imperial convoy was truly stupid, reckless, and illegal. Though his planning to marry someone’s daughters resembles besotted confusion, it remains that his words are clear. Though both Yan Zeng's and Feng Qiyan's cases appeared in the Qingdai wenziyu dang, it is difficult to consider them as "literary trials." Yan and Feng were punished harshly because they had addressed the emperor in an unsuitable manner, in both form and content. Symbolically less grave actions could be punished more lightly. request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

But before I turn to comparisons, let me first explain how the Qing legal field functioned and what kinds of documents it produced. Doing so will help to understand how the object "madness illness" (feng bing 瘋病) emerged from concrete legal practices. I then present the ways in which dynasties prior to the Qing dealt with homicides committed in a fit of insanity. $ ^{3} $ Then I probe the origin of the term "madness illness" by which Qing law

In a “re-trial” (覆審), the prefect reviewed the entire case and could go as far as to re-interrogate the accused and all the witnesses. $ ^{5} $ Even when he agreed with the magistrate’s verdict, the prefect still had to write his own report and send it to the provincial judge, the highest legal official in the province. After having made sure that the sentence meted out was consistent with the evidence presented, the judge passed the case to the governor or
who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions.

governor-general, who, after another review (usually made by his legal secretaries), transcribed all the evidence into a tiben 題本, a “routine memorial” that he sent to the Grand Secretariat (Neige 內閣), which forwarded it to the Ministry of Punishments for final confirmation. $ ^{6} $ In a new memorial, the “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingke 刑科) sometimes rejected (bo 駁), but usually confirmed the sentence proposed by the governor. $ ^{7} $ These “routine memorials” recounted entire incidents—from before the killing to the arrest—and the way they were processed by the judicial system. They even cited depositions from physicians or the relatives of mad killers, and even from the very men who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions.

governor-general, who, after another review (usually made by his legal secretaries), transcribed all the evidence into a tiben 題本, a “routine memorial” that he sent to the Grand Secretariat (Neige 內閣), which forwarded it to the Ministry of Punishments for final confirmation. $ ^{6} $ In a new memorial, the “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingke 刑科) sometimes rejected (bo 駁), but usually confirmed the sentence proposed by the governor. $ ^{7} $ These “routine memorials” recounted entire incidents—from before the killing to the arrest—and the way they were processed by the judicial system. They even cited depositions from physicians or the relatives of mad killers, and even from the very men who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions.

As has become customary in English-language scholarship, I will refer to Qing legal articles by their numbers in Huang Jingjia's 黄靜嘉 1970 re-edition of Xue Yunsheng's 薛允升 Lingering Doubts in the Study of the Sub-Statutes (Duli cunyi 讀例存疑; 1905). Xue's work was originally not an edition of the code, but an unpublished critical commentary on the "sub-statutes" (例) as they existed after 1870, when the Qing Code had last been overhauled. $ ^{9} $

Whereas in English law acts committed because of insanity did not count as crimes, Chinese imperial law sentenced killings committed because of madness differently depending on the circumstances. $ ^{10} $ For the larger part of the Han dynasty (206 BCE – 220 CE), people who killed in a state of insanity were sentenced to death. Only in the early second century CE

No law concerning mad homicides existed during the Tang dynasty (617-908). Instead, a statute called “Those Who Are Aged, Juvenile, or Disabled” (老少廢疾) granted limited clemency to “critically disabled” 篤疾 people (including mad people 癲狂) who committed crimes. When mad persons committed a homicide that deserved the death penalty, their sentence (decapitation or strangulation) had to be set forth in a special memorial to the emperor to petition for his decision. $ ^{12} $ How the emperor treated such cases is unknown. The Song dynasty (960-1279) adopted the Tang approach wholesale. $ ^{13} $

The key to the widening use of feng 瘋 to mean “madness” was its phonetic equivalence with feng 風 (“Wind”) and the age-old association of the sound feng with madness in expressions like fengkuang (風狂, “Wind madness”), fengdian (風癲, “Wind dian”), and xin feng 心風 (“Heart Wind”). By the twelfth century the sound feng (“Wind”) had been associated with madness for so long that it had come to mean madness even when it was not followed by either kuang or dian nor disambiguated by the addition of the “illness radical” (疒) to yield the form 瘋. $ ^{22} $ One “crazy monk brother” (風僧哥) was mentioned in a twelfth-century collection of anecdotes. $ ^{23} $ The term fa feng 發風 (“fit of Wind” = “fit of madness”) also appeared in a Yuan play titled A Pair of Battling Quacks (Shuang dou yi 雙鬥醫). $ ^{24} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: # 10 ## Prologue to Part Two Madness and mad people in the Qing dynasty have been studied mostly in a legal context. $ ^{1} $ The easy availability and relative intelligibility of legal documents vis-à-vis medical ones have undoubtedly contributed to this trend. $ ^{2} $ Following pioneers like Chen Hsiu-fen and Angelika Messner, I showed in Part One that Qing medical approaches to madness were also a legitimate topic. In Part Two, I want to show that the study of Qing legislative and judicial activities surrounding madness can be improved, and gains from being compared with the many other ways in which mad acts, words, and people were handled in both technical and non-technical contexts. In this prologue and the next few chapters, I will often propose brief comparisons with the medical field, leaving more systematic reflections for Chapter 16, the general conclusion. But before I turn to comparisons, let me first explain how the Qing legal field functioned and what kinds of documents it produced. Doing so will help to understand how the object "madness illness" (feng bing 瘋病) emerged from concrete legal practices. I then present the ways in which dynasties prior to the Qing dealt with homicides committed in a fit of insanity. $ ^{3} $ Then I probe the origin of the term "madness illness" by which Qing law

designated madness, but I reject the possibility that feng bing referred to another kind of madness than dian 癲 and kuang 狂. ## The Qing judicial system and the documents it produced Criminal investigations usually began when someone brought an illegal act to the attention of the local magistrate, who, in addition to being the highest-ranked local administrator, was also in charge of judicial matters. When a suspicious death occurred, the magistrate brought a coroner with him to examine the corpse and ascertain the cause of death. If necessary, the magistrate also sent deputies to apprehend the killer. Although the magistrate could start to interrogate witnesses at once, the formal trial only started when the “criminal”—as a person under trial was called—and the witnesses had all been assembled. In Chinese imperial law, no indictment told a person what charges he or she faced. Instead, the magistrate investigated the “circumstances” (qing 情) of a criminal act and then decided under what law that crime should be sentenced. He then composed a report (xiang 詳) recounting the entire affair and specifying the sentence (ni 擬) that went with the law that covered this particular act. $ ^{4} $ He sent this report to his immediate superior the prefect. In a “re-trial” (覆審), the prefect reviewed the entire case and could go as far as to re-interrogate the accused and all the witnesses. $ ...

governor-general, who, after another review (usually made by his legal secretaries), transcribed all the evidence into a tiben 題本, a “routine memorial” that he sent to the Grand Secretariat (Neige 內閣), which forwarded it to the Ministry of Punishments for final confirmation. $ ^{6} $ In a new memorial, the “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingke 刑科) sometimes rejected (bo 駁), but usually confirmed the sentence proposed by the governor. $ ^{7} $ These “routine memorials” recounted entire incidents—from before the killing to the arrest—and the way they were processed by the judicial system. They even cited depositions from physicians or the relatives of mad killers, and even from the very men who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions. But routine memorials have limits as sources of information. Since they were written only after a sentence had been meted out, the information they presented was designed to lead seamlessly to the proposed verdict. These highly structured reports left no room for debate, unless an official from the Ministry of Punishments had raised a point that required debate. When doubts were expressed, it was usually to show that they had already been resolved. The limits of these documents depend on the information we seek from them. They may provide little information on the daily life of “ordin ...

Created in the late seventeenth century, palace memorials exploded in numbers after the creation of the Grand Council during the Yongzheng reign (1723-1735). The Grand Council, which unlike the Grand Secretariat worked in direct collaboration with the monarch, became the most important agency in the central government from the 1730s until the end of the Qing. Legal cases judged grave (like parricide or sedition) had to be reported to the Grand Council by palace memorial. Such cases were processed more quickly than those reported through routine memorials, because palace memorials were considered more urgent, and because the sentences meted out in them necessitated no repeated reviews. Unlike tiben, palace memorials could also contain documentary attachments, such as seized texts or more detailed transcriptions of depositions. The cases I discuss in Chapters 14 and 15 were all brought to the attention of the throne through palace memorials. Besides “routine” and “palace” memorials, I also found information on Qing official views of madness in successive editions of the Qing Code (called Dai Qing liü 大清律, Dai Qing liü jijie fuli 大清律集解附例, or Dai Qing lüli 大清律例 depending on when they were published), commentaries on the Code, imperial edicts, memorials on policy issues, as well as several collections of legal cases. As has become customary in English-language scholarship, I will re ...

Lingering Doubts contained neither the diagrams (on family relations, legal instruments of punishment, technical terms, table of monetary redemption, etc.) included at the beginning of the official editions of the Code, nor the running commentaries that topped each page in both private and state-sponsored editions of the Code. It is Huang Jingjia's inclusion of these tables and his numbering of all the statutes and sub-statutes that make his edition (not a "reprint") of Lingering Doubts so convenient. Xue's analyses themselves are invaluable for tracing the origin of most sub-statutes, though he was imprecise about Kangxi-era (1662-1722) legislation. ## The pre-Qing handling of mad homicides Let me now discuss pre-Qing legal treatments of mad homicides and trace at the same time the emergence of the character feng 瘋, by which Qing law officially referred to insanity. The terms by which "madness" was designated in Chinese imperial law changed through time, yet no word in particular implied either distance from or closeness with medical approaches. The legal treatment of mad people under dynasties that used medical terms like diankuang 顛狂 and fengkuang 風狂 seems to have been no more (or less) compatible with medical approaches than that of eras that used less medical concepts like xinfeng 心風 or kuangyi 狂易. Whereas in English law acts committed because of insanity did not count as ...

were mad killers granted an unspecified reduction of punishment (狂易殺人 待減重論). $ ^{11} $ No law concerning mad homicides existed during the Tang dynasty (617-908). Instead, a statute called “Those Who Are Aged, Juvenile, or Disabled” (老少廢疾) granted limited clemency to “critically disabled” 篤疾 people (including mad people 癲狂) who committed crimes. When mad persons committed a homicide that deserved the death penalty, their sentence (decapitation or strangulation) had to be set forth in a special memorial to the emperor to petition for his decision. $ ^{12} $ How the emperor treated such cases is unknown. The Song dynasty (960-1279) adopted the Tang approach wholesale. $ ^{13} $

Unlike the Tang and Song, the Yuan dynasty (1260-1368) promulgated several explicit laws concerning homicides committed by mad people. Yuan courts were instructed to diminish the sentence of mad killers, but not to absolve them from penalties entirely. For instance, a man who killed one of his parents or grand-parents in a state of insanity (風狂) was to be simply “executed” (處死) rather than put to death by dismemberment. $ ^{14} $ In 1269 a man killed four people in a fit of “Heart Wind” (心風). $ ^{15} $ Legal officials petitioned his case to the emperor (上請) “in accordance with the old precedent” (照依舊例) that instructed to do so in cases of killing because of insanity (顛狂殺人). $ ^{16} $ Khubilai Khan eventually absolved the killer from the death penalty by imperial edict (敕裁) and condemned him to paying 50 ounces of silver (per person?) in “cremation or burial fees” (燒埋銀) to the family of his victims, the same amount as for people who committed a regular homicide. $ ^{17} $ Khubilai's reasoning may have been the same as the one behind another Yuan law stating that someone who, in a state of madness (病風狂), injured another person during a fight, leading to that person's death (毆傷人致死), should be "absolved of crime" (免罪),

though cremation or burial fees should still be collected (徵燒埋銀) from the culprit (History of the Yuan 105: 2678). Early Qing laws handled mad homicides in a similar fashion, though the legal reasoning that justified monetary compensation differed. $ ^{18} $ Another Yuan law absolved those who “spoke inappropriately” (失口亂言) as long as they had done so while “suffering from Wind madness” (害風狂疾) and if there was nothing else behind their words. $ ^{19} $ As I will show in Chapters 14 and 15, the Qing attitude toward seditious words pronounced by mad people would be much harsher. Yuan legal documents referred to madness as xin feng 心風 (“Heart Wind”), feng kuang 風狂 (“Wind kuang”), dian kuang 顛狂 (“insanity”), and perhaps even feng bing 瘋病, the term that Qing law would officially adopt to discuss insanity. $ ^{20} $ Despite their different origins, these names did not have different legal implications. $ ^{21} $

cluster.chapter_title: Mad acts, mad speech, and mad people in Chinese medicine and law

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Ng’s reasoning rests on two Qing developments: 1) in the 1730s, the government ordered families to confine their mad relatives even when they had committed no crime; 2) in the eighteenth century, legislators meted out increasingly severe sentences upon those who killed someone in a fit of madness. Ng argued that these measures, which she attributed to a more-assumed-than-argued Manchu obsession with social control, represented a sea change in Chinese attitudes toward madness. But the “from illness to deviance” thesis is largely an artifact of the way Ng staged her documents. $ ^{4} $ When Ng claims that legal views replaced medical ones, it is in fact her analytical gaze that leaps from medical to legal documents, never to look back. As I shall show in chapters 11 to 15, medical, legal, religious, and popular views often overlapped, and they kept interacting in concrete social contexts throughout Qing times. Even Qing law consistently treated insanity as an illness.

I would like to study shifting conceptions and practices without presuming that they were hemmed around any kind of “madness in itself,” however ungrasable. But without an ontological coagulant, what are we studying when we claim to be writing the history of “madness”?¹⁶ Harnessing Foucault’s (1976) critique of the notion of “sexuality,” we could say that “madness” brings together in an artificial unity a disparate array of notions, people, ways of speaking, and forms of behavior.¹⁷ I also find these words by Foucault suggestive: “I have personally never written that madness does not exist, but this could be written, because, for phenomenology, madness exists but it is not a thing, whereas we should say on the contrary that madness does not exist, but that it is not nothing for that reason (mais qu’elle n’est pas rien pour autant).”¹⁸ Words have referents even if these are not natural objects. One of my aims will be to highlight this artificial unity of “madness” and to refashion the resulting dispersion

I would like to study shifting conceptions and practices without presuming that they were hemmed around any kind of “madness in itself,” however ungrasable. But without an ontological coagulant, what are we studying when we claim to be writing the history of “madness”?¹⁶ Harnessing Foucault’s (1976) critique of the notion of “sexuality,” we could say that “madness” brings together in an artificial unity a disparate array of notions, people, ways of speaking, and forms of behavior.¹⁷ I also find these words by Foucault suggestive: “I have personally never written that madness does not exist, but this could be written, because, for phenomenology, madness exists but it is not a thing, whereas we should say on the contrary that madness does not exist, but that it is not nothing for that reason (mais qu’elle n’est pas rien pour autant).”¹⁸ Words have referents even if these are not natural objects. One of my aims will be to highlight this artificial unity of “madness” and to refashion the resulting dispersion

Nouns are the Trojan horses of ontology. “A substantive misleads one into looking for a substance. One populates the world with ethereal beings, the shadowy companions of the substantive. One could legitimately call the science of these fictitious entities metaphysics.” $ ^{19} $ Speaking of “representations of madness,” for instance, implies that people observed something called “madness” and then created representations of “it.” But there is no need to identify a “shadowy companion”—a substance—behind the substantive “madness” before we go further. What counts as madness in any culture is defined socially, expressed through cultural idioms, and handled in culturally meaningful fashion. No timeless and pre-cultural “madness” (were we even able to pinpoint such a thing) would suffice to make these representations and practices intelligible. $ ^{20} $

Nouns are the Trojan horses of ontology. “A substantive misleads one into looking for a substance. One populates the world with ethereal beings, the shadowy companions of the substantive. One could legitimately call the science of these fictitious entities metaphysics.” $ ^{19} $ Speaking of “representations of madness,” for instance, implies that people observed something called “madness” and then created representations of “it.” But there is no need to identify a “shadowy companion”—a substance—behind the substantive “madness” before we go further. What counts as madness in any culture is defined socially, expressed through cultural idioms, and handled in culturally meaningful fashion. No timeless and pre-cultural “madness” (were we even able to pinpoint such a thing) would suffice to make these representations and practices intelligible. $ ^{20} $

What makes comparisons possible is that Chinese people—experts and commoners alike—identified as mad the same disorders of behavior, speech, affect, and perception that we usually include in an intuitive category of “madness.” $ ^{21} $ The legal and medical practices I study often relied on this self-evidence, on people’s intuition that some acts naturally deserved to be called kuang, dian, or feng, three words that usually meant “mad” or “acting madly” in the same vague sense as the English words I just translated them by.

To avoid making generalizations that will predictably not apply to all people of all periods, I use the notion of “prototype.” $ ^{22} $ Most definitions are prototypical in that they mark out the most typical members of a given category (“tables,” “burrowing animals”). Categories contain more than prototypes, but their members gradually radiate away from the prototypical hub. A three-legged chair is thus a less prototypical chair than a four-legged one, the platypus a less prototypical mammal than the elephant.

In the interrelated ways in which they explained and handled madness, the three contemporaneous fields of homicide law, medicine, and trials for sedition form a manifold cultural formation, a network of discourses and practices that is most profitably studied as a whole if we want to appreciate what any of them meant. $ ^{23} $ To understand why Qing law discussed insanity and handled “mad criminals” the way it did, we need to compare it to the medical and religious approaches that were also current at the time. And we shall appreciate the nature of medical treatments all the better if we grasp the ways in which other fields handled mad behavior.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: ### 1.1 Historiography: insights and oversights Madness has just begun to attract the attention of historians of China. Vivien Ng's Madness in Late Imperial China (1990), until recently the only book on this topic, examined the way mad people were handled in Qing society and how their treatment changed over time. Ng was the first scholar to dissect a number of legal cases in which madness played a role, but the book's main thesis—that madness was transformed "from an illness to a form of criminal deviance" in the eighteenth century—is unpersuasive. Ng’s reasoning rests on two Qing developments: 1) in the 1730s, the government ordered families to confine their mad relatives even when they had committed no crime; 2) in the eighteenth century, legislators meted out increasingly severe sentences upon those who killed someone in a fit of madness. Ng argued that these measures, which she attributed to a more-assumed-than-argued Manchu obsession with social control, represented a sea change in Chinese attitudes toward madness. But the “from illness to deviance” thesis is largely an artifact of the way Ng staged her documents. $ ^{4} $ When Ng claims that legal views replaced medical ones, it is in fact her analytical gaze that leaps from medical to legal documents, never to look back. As I shall show in chapters 11 to 15, medical, legal, religious, and popular views often overlapped, ...

of madness” (expériences de la folie) that appeared so often in Foucault’s book also seemed to imply a common object to all these experiences. “Madness” in History of Madness thus meant at least three things: a) the “notions, institutions, juridical and police measures, and scientific concepts” that created historically shifting experiences of madness; b) the Ding an sich (“madness itself”)—carefully black-boxed by Foucault—that tied this ensemble together; and c) (crucially for Foucault’s Kantian critique of subject-centered philosophies) the entity the silencing of which was the transcendental condition of possibility of all the “experiences” discussed in the book. This tension is probably part of what still makes Foucault’s book uniquely attractive. I would like to study shifting conceptions and practices without presuming that they were hemmed around any kind of “madness in itself,” however ungrasable. But without an ontological coagulant, what are we studying when we claim to be writing the history of “madness”?¹⁶ Harnessing Foucault’s (1976) critique of the notion of “sexuality,” we could say that “madness” brings together in an artificial unity a disparate array of notions, people, ways of speaking, and forms of behavior.¹⁷ I also find these words by Foucault suggestive: “I have personally never written that madness does not exist, but this could be written, because, for ...

into an intelligible research object. Throughout, I shall retain Foucault's sensitivity to how specific historical objects emerge, and to how discourses take roots in social practices. ### 1.3 Substances and their referents: “what is madness?” and what is “madness”? Meaning and referentiality are immensely difficult philosophical issues that I will not attempt to solve. But deflecting metaphysical questions is a pre-condition for understanding how referents emerge historically—that is, how practices attach words to specific realities. Nouns are the Trojan horses of ontology. “A substantive misleads one into looking for a substance. One populates the world with ethereal beings, the shadowy companions of the substantive. One could legitimately call the science of these fictitious entities metaphysics.” $ ^{19} $ Speaking of “representations of madness,” for instance, implies that people observed something called “madness” and then created representations of “it.” But there is no need to identify a “shadowy companion”—a substance—behind the substantive “madness” before we go further. What counts as madness in any culture is defined socially, expressed through cultural idioms, and handled in culturally meaningful fashion. No timeless and pre-cultural “madness” (were we even able to pinpoint such a thing) would suffice to make these representations and practices intelligible. $ ^{20 ...

“acts, words, or people” because documents rarely problematized madness as a whole, and because making such a distinction sidesteps the distracting question of what madness is. “Madness” did not precede the representations that people made of “it” (remember the longhand). And what held together demonic possession, postpartum confusion, and furious fits occasioned by high fevers was not a common ontological substrate, but notions that Qing people found intelligibly related: mainly kuang 狂, feng 瘋, and dian 顛／癲. None of these terms was exclusive to any field. Ritual specialists used them; so did doctors and legislators. And most texts used these words to label similar symptoms. What makes comparisons possible is that Chinese people—experts and commoners alike—identified as mad the same disorders of behavior, speech, affect, and perception that we usually include in an intuitive category of “madness.” $ ^{21} $ The legal and medical practices I study often relied on this self-evidence, on people’s intuition that some acts naturally deserved to be called kuang, dian, or feng, three words that usually meant “mad” or “acting madly” in the same vague sense as the English words I just translated them by. I will still strive to understand what each of these terms meant, but I will not do so in the abstract. We could apply to “madness” what Ludwig Wittgenstein said about “time”: “If I kn ...

statements meaningful and consequential. A “script” is a scenario according to which a process—e.g., the course of an illness or a crime—was understood or expected to unfold. These scripts were templates for describing reality, but also interpretive frameworks that guided people’s interventions in fluid processes. Physicians deployed such scripts when they treated patients, judicial officials when they sentenced crimes. To avoid making generalizations that will predictably not apply to all people of all periods, I use the notion of “prototype.” $ ^{22} $ Most definitions are prototypical in that they mark out the most typical members of a given category (“tables,” “burrowing animals”). Categories contain more than prototypes, but their members gradually radiate away from the prototypical hub. A three-legged chair is thus a less prototypical chair than a four-legged one, the platypus a less prototypical mammal than the elephant. Since prototypes also apply to the way we interpret events, I will call “prototypical scripts” the recurring scenarios by which experts explained how illnesses or crimes took place. The script “internal depletion occurs → Wind intrudes → illness ensues,” for instance, was omnipresent in medical treatises from the seventh and eighth centuries (see 3.3). New scripts emphasizing the pathology of mucus gradually replaced Wind-based ones and came to dominate ...

evidence also exists about how madness was handled in less literate social circles, often but not always through religious ritual. In the interrelated ways in which they explained and handled madness, the three contemporaneous fields of homicide law, medicine, and trials for sedition form a manifold cultural formation, a network of discourses and practices that is most profitably studied as a whole if we want to appreciate what any of them meant. $ ^{23} $ To understand why Qing law discussed insanity and handled “mad criminals” the way it did, we need to compare it to the medical and religious approaches that were also current at the time. And we shall appreciate the nature of medical treatments all the better if we grasp the ways in which other fields handled mad behavior. Since madness was never an important focus of medical reflections, changes in medical views of madness are difficult to study. Kuang was the object of no specialized literature, and no debate concerning it had any major impact on doctrinal developments. But since late imperial physicians were expected to treat all disorders—from furor to furuncles—the way they handled any illness usually depended on how they understood pathogenesis, vitality, and general therapeutic principles. I posit that changes in approaches to kuang can be explained only if we understand broader transformations in medical thinking. The ...

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $

Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $

To assess and explain this diversity properly, I pay attention not only to trendsetters and famous doctors, but also to writings that epitomized their age more than they marked it: $ ^{3} $ primers, florilegia of medical oddities, unpublished collections of medical cases, etc. In addition to painting a more complete image of medical views of madness, this broad

Categories and scripts channeled phenomena into intelligible and manageable forms; they also guided perception and suggested methods to tackle the reality they purported to describe. Broadly speaking, medical specialists attributed mad symptoms either to invasion by external forces (“deviant Qi”, Wind, ghosts) or to internal imbalances strong enough to provoke illness. $ ^{7} $ Doctors claimed they could cure their patients by expelling these external invaders or redressing these pathogenic imbalances. Because of this emphasis on dynamic situations and a general therapeutic optimism, the medical tradition considered permanent disorders anomalous. Chinese physicians therefore rarely medicalized personality and almost never called patients “madmen” (狂人 or 瘋子).

I start by examining the prototypical symptoms of kuang as defined in the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (first century BCE), a pivotal corpus from which medical writers drew their descriptions of madness for most of imperial history. I then criticize the way modern scholars have used "mental illness" as a foil against which to define Chinese approaches, or even as a reason to refuse comparisons between psychiatry and Chinese medicine. As I shall argue, Chinese medicine and modern psychiatry are comparable because both purport to explain and treat psycho-behavioral symptoms. As I explain next, Chinese doctors assumed that mental activities "supervened" upon organic processes. $ ^{1} $ This is the most critical feature of Chinese medical approaches to insanity. I then map the relations between kuang and proximate illnesses in a seventh-century treatise that attributed most psycho-behavioral pathologies to harmful Winds. The fourth section explains how dian (瑱, 顛, or 癲)—"seizure sickness"—came to refer to insanity by borrowing from non-medical usage.

Modern scholars agree that the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (Huangdi neijing 黄帝内經) was "an unintegrated compilation of short writings" (Sivin 1995f: 191). Its received versions—the Grand Basis, the Basic Questions, and the Divine Pivot, all of which date to the seventh century or later but were compiled from sources as early as the third century BCE²—were "simply different collections of similar material. Any one collection therefore represented the end of one lineage of collecting, as if frozen in time" (Lo 2002: xxix).

explanations and doctrinal statements. $ ^{3} $ Elite physicians from the eleventh to the twentieth century proclaimed it should form the core of medical education. The diversity of views contained in the Canon allowed a number of rival doctrines hinging on it to claim authenticity.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Throughout, I use “illness” as a translation of the term bing 病 and as a label for the kinds of disorders that Chinese medicine discussed: clusters of clinically relevant symptoms that were grouped under one particular name. The problem in translating bing is not to determine whether that term really meant “illness,” “disease,” or “disorder,” but to find a rendering that neither clashes with the meaning of bing nor brings undue connotations into our analysis. To some extent, a translation is good because of what it avoids. Translating bing as “illness” will not presuppose that all bing defined in Chinese medical texts were “diseases,” a term that now seems to imply rootedness in biological processes. I also avoid “disorder” because it would make Chinese concepts of madness sound too close to those of the DSM (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders), the influential manual that calls most psychiatric derangements “disorders” (Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder and the like). $ ^{4} $
