page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: ## 2 Prologue to Part One Diversity, coherence, and scheme transfer Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $ To assess and explain this diversity properly, I pay attention not only to trendsetters and famous doctors, but also to writings that epitomized their age more than they marked it: $ ^{3} $ primers, florilegia of medical oddities, unpublished collections of medical cases, etc. In additio ...

approach also deemphasizes the contrast between “elite medicine” and “popular practice” by showing that a large range of literate practitioners lived between these two abstract extremes. My broad reading of medical texts will also help to show that although madness was rarely at the core of doctrinal controversies, medical discussions of insanity quickly absorbed new doctrinal developments. The seven chapters of Part One will thus narrate how conceptions of kuang and its related illnesses evolved along with the rest of medical precepts and practices. Throughout, I use “illness” as a translation of the term bing 病 and as a label for the kinds of disorders that Chinese medicine discussed: clusters of clinically relevant symptoms that were grouped under one particular name. The problem in translating bing is not to determine whether that term really meant “illness,” “disease,” or “disorder,” but to find a rendering that neither clashes with the meaning of bing nor brings undue connotations into our analysis. To some extent, a translation is good because of what it avoids. Translating bing as “illness” will not presuppose that all bing defined in Chinese medical texts were “diseases,” a term that now seems to imply rootedness in biological processes. I also avoid “disorder” because it would make Chinese concepts of madness sound too close to those of the DSM (Diagnostic and Statist ...

task. Yet case records remind us that illness meanings were more than organic. As an affliction that was defined socially, insanity was always more than a bodily event. $ ^{6} $ Categories and scripts channeled phenomena into intelligible and manageable forms; they also guided perception and suggested methods to tackle the reality they purported to describe. Broadly speaking, medical specialists attributed mad symptoms either to invasion by external forces (“deviant Qi”, Wind, ghosts) or to internal imbalances strong enough to provoke illness. $ ^{7} $ Doctors claimed they could cure their patients by expelling these external invaders or redressing these pathogenic imbalances. Because of this emphasis on dynamic situations and a general therapeutic optimism, the medical tradition considered permanent disorders anomalous. Chinese physicians therefore rarely medicalized personality and almost never called patients “madmen” (狂人 or 瘋子). The medical scripts by which doctors understood madness changed significantly between the eighth and the fourteenth century, and again from the fourteenth to the sixteenth century. I plan to demonstrate that new explanations and treatments for insanity often borrowed from the prototypical scenarios of other illnesses or from contemporaneous interpretations of human vitality rather than from the observation of mad patients. A formal analysis would sh ...

Psycho-behavioral pathology in Chinese medicine Concepts and early developments, $ 3^{rd} $ century BCE – ca. 1150 This chapter provides conceptual and factual tools for understanding how medical treatments of insanity changed despite how stably Chinese doctors kept describing mad behavior. As I pointed out in Chapter 1, Chinese people called “mad” the same disorders of behavior, speech, affect, and perception that we intuitively include under the category of madness. Here I survey the way ancient medical texts described such symptoms and analyze how physicians explained and treated psycho-behavioral pathology before about 1150. I start by examining the prototypical symptoms of kuang as defined in the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (first century BCE), a pivotal corpus from which medical writers drew their descriptions of madness for most of imperial history. I then criticize the way modern scholars have used "mental illness" as a foil against which to define Chinese approaches, or even as a reason to refuse comparisons between psychiatry and Chinese medicine. As I shall argue, Chinese medicine and modern psychiatry are comparable because both purport to explain and treat psycho-behavioral symptoms. As I explain next, Chinese doctors assumed that mental activities "supervened" upon organic processes. $ ^{1} $ This is the most critical feature of Chinese medical approaches to insa ...

The second half of this chapter traces how the growing prominence of mucus as a cause of illness starting in the eleventh century deeply altered the way in which Chinese physicians understood and handled madness. I first describe the origins of the notion of “mucus,” a pathological fluid that was not mentioned in the Inner Canon. I then review how the medical activism and publishing activities of the northern-Song government (976-1127) helped to create a pool of common references that facilitated medical debates and intertextual borrowings. For example, many Song doctors debated the claim made in a government-sponsored formulary that “Wind strokes” could be treated with vomit-inducing (“emetic”) drugs. Just around the same time (the turn of the twelfth century), these mucus-targeting emetics were starting to be used to treat madness. ### 3.1 The kuang 狂 core in the Yellow Emperor corpus Modern scholars agree that the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (Huangdi neijing 黄帝内經) was "an unintegrated compilation of short writings" (Sivin 1995f: 191). Its received versions—the Grand Basis, the Basic Questions, and the Divine Pivot, all of which date to the seventh century or later but were compiled from sources as early as the third century BCE²—were "simply different collections of similar material. Any one collection therefore represented the end of one lineage of collecting, as if frozen ...

explanations and doctrinal statements. $ ^{3} $ Elite physicians from the eleventh to the twentieth century proclaimed it should form the core of medical education. The diversity of views contained in the Canon allowed a number of rival doctrines hinging on it to claim authenticity. One influential passage on madness in the Inner Canon has been traced to the earlier Cauterization Canon of the Eleven Yin and Yang Vessels (Yinyang shiyi mai jiu jing 陰陽十一脈灸 經), a work of the late third century BCE that described the disorders that occurred when eleven distinct vessels (脈) “stirred” or “were stirred” (動).⁴ A few lines on the “Yang- Brilliance” (陽明) vessel later found their way into a section of the Basic Questions that later physicians liked to cite when they described kuang.⁵ When this [vessel] is moved, one ails from: shivers and ailing from coldness; fondness for stretching; continual yawning, blackness in the center of the forehead; ailing from swelling; when severe, detestation for people and fire; when hearing the sound of wood, panicked alarm; panicky heart; the desire to close doors and windows and dwell in isolation; when the ailment is severe, the desire to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run. These constitute shin reversal. For these [ailments], the Yang-Brilliance vessel controls the treatment.

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Was madness a single object, then? And were words like diankuang and jengbing all synonyms? No. Although both legal and medical specialists counted on the easy recognizability of madness, they transposed daily practice and local labels into specialized categories and scripts. $ ^{2} $ Penal denominations and illness names channeled phenomena into understandable and manageable forms. Physicians, for instance, tended to reduce to organic imbalances some problems that had probably emerged through social interactions. They asserted their competence by claiming to understand the organic roots of emotional discomforts and behavioral disorders. This reductionism created—or “enacted”—a different kind of madness than the one found in ritual therapies or judicial practice. $ ^{3} $ As such, physicians faced a different object than legal officials.

Was madness a single object, then? And were words like diankuang and jengbing all synonyms? No. Although both legal and medical specialists counted on the easy recognizability of madness, they transposed daily practice and local labels into specialized categories and scripts. $ ^{2} $ Penal denominations and illness names channeled phenomena into understandable and manageable forms. Physicians, for instance, tended to reduce to organic imbalances some problems that had probably emerged through social interactions. They asserted their competence by claiming to understand the organic roots of emotional discomforts and behavioral disorders. This reductionism created—or “enacted”—a different kind of madness than the one found in ritual therapies or judicial practice. $ ^{3} $ As such, physicians faced a different object than legal officials.

Legal officials did not understand madness in the same way as physicians. Although the legal object “madness illness” borrowed from medical and popular conceptions, it evolved to suit the concerns and categories of the legal field. At first, Qing law targeted only one kind of act (homicides), not madness or mad people in general. A killing was considered to have been committed “because of madness” when the killer had had no cognizance—and had no recollection—of his act. $ ^{4} $ This lack of awareness was what convinced early Qing officials that mad killers should not be physically punished. $ ^{5} $

Legal officials did not understand madness in the same way as physicians. Although the legal object “madness illness” borrowed from medical and popular conceptions, it evolved to suit the concerns and categories of the legal field. At first, Qing law targeted only one kind of act (homicides), not madness or mad people in general. A killing was considered to have been committed “because of madness” when the killer had had no cognizance—and had no recollection—of his act. $ ^{4} $ This lack of awareness was what convinced early Qing officials that mad killers should not be physically punished. $ ^{5} $

Fiction could be put to many more uses. One feature of mad people in medical texts and popular perception was the peculiarity of their words. How did fictional sources portray mad speech? My impression is that mad characters in Chinese stories were usually misrecognized as mad before being revealed to be something else—playful immortals in disguise, hermits or visionaries, or people in the grip of real demons. Madness thus served to dissimulate something else, sometimes from the reader, but most often from other characters in the story. $ ^{6} $

Although the legal object “madness illness” borrowed from medical and popular conceptions, it evolved to suit the concerns and categories of the legal field. At first, Qing law targeted only one kind of act (homicides), not madness or mad people in general. A killing was considered to have been committed “because of madness” when the killer had had no cognizance—and had no recollection—of his act. $ ^{4} $ This lack of awareness was what convinced early Qing officials that mad killers should not be physically punished. $ ^{5} $ When the Ministry of Punishments ordered all families to confine “people with madness illness” preventively (first in 1689, and then more rigorously in the 1730s and 1760s), it was thinking of madness from the point of view of its occasional outcome—a homicide committed during a fit (see 11.4). The apparent danger of insanity had been made obvious by the very law that treated mad killers leniently, because each application of this law showed that someone had killed “because of madness,” and bec ...

probably find more information in fiction—Katz did mention some eighteenth-century stories—on how families treated their mad relatives, in the same way as Edward Davis (2001) used a collection of stories to reconstruct the nature of Song local religion. Fiction could be put to many more uses. One feature of mad people in medical texts and popular perception was the peculiarity of their words. How did fictional sources portray mad speech? My impression is that mad characters in Chinese stories were usually misrecognized as mad before being revealed to be something else—playful immortals in disguise, hermits or visionaries, or people in the grip of real demons. Madness thus served to dissimulate something else, sometimes from the reader, but most often from other characters in the story. $ ^{6} $ Another issue I barely addressed is how educated people dealt with their own mental torments. What did men who resorted to neither medicine nor exorcism do when they felt themselves becoming crazy or saw their peers lose sanity? We could probably find several cases like that of Xu Nianzu 許念祖 (1655-1698), a scholar who committed suicide after repeated bouts of despair that his friends and correspondents referred to as madness (for references, see 8.5, p. 344n50).
